title: Announcements

text:
### Introduction

The Centre for Technology Enhanced Learning ("CTEL") is part of King's College London ("King's", "the university", "we", "us", or "our"). If you have any questions about this notice, please contact CTEL: [ctel@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:ctel@kcl.ac.uk)

This privacy notice supplements the following wider privacy notices: [King's College London Core Privacy Notice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/terms/privacy) , [Student Data Collection Notice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/professional-services/business-assurance/student-data-collection-notice), [Human Resources Privacy Notice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/professional-services/hr/privacy-notice "https://www.kcl.ac.uk/professional-services/hr/privacy-notice") and the [Prospective Student Data Collection Notice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/terms/prospective-student-data-collection). All data is processed in compliance with UK data protection legislation and College governance.

#### About KEATS

[KEATS (King's E-learning and Teaching Service)](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/) is the university's Virtual Learning Environment (VLE) available to all students, faculties/departments across the university.

### What information we collect and how we use it

We collect and process your personal data when you enrol to study at King's and during your student journey.

We process your name, email address (either King's or other dependent upon course type), and your user ID to enable us to set you up with an account and to deliver course content, manage and administer individual course progress reporting, assignments, assessments, quiz results, and other learning activities to you within KEATS.

We use your data to review engagement with your studies and to monitor your attendance and usage of KEATS including action types such as the pages you visit, your IP address and the time stamp of activity to ensure that both you and King's are meeting respective obligations set out in our Student Attendance and Engagement Policy.

We use Google Analytics and Microsoft Clarity, both web analytics services, to understand how visitors engage with KEATS and so we can improve it.

We also use your data to review course content performance and identify where improvements can be made to enhance the learning experience for students.

We may use your contact information to send important notifications, updates, and information about your courses.

### Our legal basis for processing your information
title: KEATS Privacy Statement

text:
[Context: Our legal basis for processing your information]

... use your data to review course content performance and identify where improvements can be made to enhance the learning experience for students.

We may use your contact information to send important notifications, updates, and information about your courses.

### Our legal basis for processing your information

To provide your education and deliver university services and events we need to process your data to fulfil our public task as a university and perform our contract with you. Digital education tools are used in pursuance of our legitimate interests. We rely on our legitimate Interests where the processing is for a legitimate interest of the university or a third party, and that interest is not overridden by your interests and rights such as monitoring the effectiveness of our digital learning activities.

We also process data to meet our statutory obligations such as monitoring and gathering data to meet our reporting requirements to governmental bodies.

### Who we share your information with

Our provision of an effective service at King's includes multiple internal teams that are both central such as IT, Student Operations, and faculty based such as programme managers, academics who provide support and services to you.

Your personal information will be shared by us internally within King's on a need-to-know basis and in compliance with the [King's Data Protection Policy and Procedure](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/data-protection-policy-2).

We disclose your data to third parties where they are acting as authorised agents for the university for the purposes listed below; or where we are permitted or required to do so under UK law.

Typical third party data sharing includes the following plugin and LTI (Learning Technology Interoperability) integrations which are currently in use within KEATS. Each of these tools uses some KEATS personal data as previously stated to enable access and support various functions, including grade calculations and storage, in compliance with the KEATS Privacy Notice. A privacy notice for each LTI tool can be accessed via the links below.

**Institution-wide**

* [Echo 360](https://echo360.com/privacy/#:~:text=Echo360%20will%20take%20commercially%20reasonable,to%20%E2%80%9Copt%2Dout.%E2%80%9D_) \- Lecture capture linking to KEATS module areas

* [Turnitin](https://help.turnitin.com/Privacy_and_Security/Privacy_and_Security.htm) \- Assessment tool with inbuilt plagiarism checking

* [Leganto](https://exlibrisgroup.com/ko/privacy-policy-1-2/) \- Reading lists linked in KEATS

* [Kaltura](https://corp.kaltura.com/legal/privacy/privacy-policy/) \- Video on demand (VOD) service used at King's

text:
### Introduction

This accessibility statement applies to the service KEATS (keats.kcl.ac.uk).

KEATS is the name King's uses for our Virtual Learning Environment. KEATS is comprised of a number of complementary applications. The underlying application is the third-party open-source application Moodle.

Moodle is freely distributed by [moodle.org](https://moodle.org/). King's College London uses Moodle to support learning and teaching by providing course materials and information, assessment tools, discussion forums and other features.

This statement relates only to the Moodle component of KEATS

Separate statements for the other applications are available from the [KCL Web accessibility page](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/sitehelp/accessibility):

* Echo 360 - Lecture Capture

* Evasys - Module evaluations

* Leganto - Reading Lists

* Turnitin - Plagiarism checking and assessment submission

* Mahara - King's ePortfolio

* Kaltura - Video on demand service

#### Using the application

We want as many people as possible to be able to use, read and understand the content of KEATS.

This means that you should be able to:

* change colours, contrast levels and fonts, using browser plugins

* zoom in up to 300% without the text spilling off the screen

* navigate most of the application using just a keyboard

* navigate the application using speech recognition software

* listen to most of the application using a screen reader

Due to Moodle being a third-party application, some aspects of accessibility on KEATS are outside of our immediate control.

[AbilityNet](https://mcmw.abilitynet.org.uk/) has advice on making your device easier to use if you have a disability.

### How accessible this application is

Although the underlying Moodle application is WCAG 2.1 AA compliant, we know some parts of KEATS are not fully accessible. The complexity and volume of content available presents difficulties in identifying all accessibility issues. Users may experience issues depending on the course/area they are accessing. For example:

* Many of our PDFs and Word documents don't meet accessibility standards - for example, they may not be structured so they're accessible to a screen reader. This doesn't meet WCAG 2.1 success criterion 4.1.2 (name, role value).

* Navigation using only a keyboard is possible for much of the content in KEATS, but it doesn't work for all elements; for example, some elements on KEATS pages cannot be reached using the keyboard alone.
title: Accessibility Statement

text:
[Context: How accessible this application is]

...2 (name, role value).

* Navigation using only a keyboard is possible for much of the content in KEATS, but it doesn't work for all elements; for example, some elements on KEATS pages cannot be reached using the keyboard alone.

* Some uploaded content e.g., PDFs, Word documents, PowerPoint presentations, may not be fully accessible.

* Some interactive content types e.g., SCORM and H5P may not be fully accessible.

* Our site includes third-party content and functionality. We cannot always guarantee its accessibility.

### Feedback and contact information

If you find any problems not listed in this statement or think we're not meeting accessibility requirements, email [diversity@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:diversity@kcl.ac.uk).

If you need information on this application in a different format like accessible PDF, large print, easy read, audio recording or braille, email [diversity@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:diversity@kcl.ac.uk).

We will consider your request and get back to you in 7 days.

If you need to escalate your issue further, contact Lorraine Kelly, Director of Equality, Diversity & Inclusion, via [diversity@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:diversity@kcl.ac.uk). You can expect an acknowledgement of your issue within 7 days and a full reply within 14 days. If your complaint raises complex issues that cannot be answered within 14 days, we will keep you informed of progress until we can fully respond.

#### Enforcement procedure

The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) is responsible for enforcing the Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018 (the 'accessibility regulations'). If you're not happy with how we respond to your complaint, [contact the Equality Advisory and Support Service (EASS)](https://www.equalityadvisoryservice.com/).

#### Technical information about this application's accessibility

King's College London is committed to making this application accessible, in accordance with the Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018.

#### Non-accessible content

KEATS is provided by a third party supplier which means that some aspects of accessibility are outside our immediate control. Other components added to KEATS are also provided by third parties.

The content listed below is non-accessible for the following reasons.

#### Compliance status

This application is partially compliant with the [Web Content Accessibility Guidelines version 2.1 ](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/) Level AA standard, due to the non-compliances listed below.

#### Non-compliance with the accessibility regulations
title: Accessibility Statement

text:
[Context: Feedback and contact information > Non-compliance with the accessibility regulations]

... partially compliant with the [Web Content Accessibility Guidelines version 2.1 ](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/) Level AA standard, due to the non-compliances listed below.

#### Non-compliance with the accessibility regulations

* 1.3.1 Info and relationships: Some instances of empty container elements

* 1.4.3 Contrast (minimum): Some content, mostly in "hidden" sections, does not meet the minimum requirements. This may cause difficulty for users with visual limitations.

* 2.4.6 Headings and labels: Some instances of empty headings (headings exist but contain no information). This may cause confusion to users of screen readers and other technologies.

* 2.5.3 Label in name: Some visible labels do not match their accessible names. This may cause confusion understanding and navigating the content for some users.

* 4.1.2 Name, role, value.
One inline frame (Module evaluations block) was found to have no text alternative.
Some instances of 'hidden' elements with focusable content. This may cause confusion when the focus falls on a 'hidden' element ('hidden' content is not rendered depending on the user's role in Moodle).

#### Disproportionate burden

At this time, we have not made any disproportionate burden claims.

#### Content that's not within the scope of the accessibility regulations

##### PDFs and other documents

The accessibility regulations do not require us to fix PDFs or other documents published before 23 September 2018 if they're not essential to providing our services. However, for files uploaded to KEATS (e.g., PDFs, Microsoft Office documents and other resources) the Blackboard Ally plugin on the KEATS platform provides the ability to download the content in alternative formats.

Any new PDFs or Word documents we publish will meet accessibility standards.

##### Live video

Live video is covered in the accessibility statements for Echo 360 and Kaltura.

#### What we are doing to improve accessibility

We are improving the accessibility of online teaching materials: providing guidance and training to staff publishing content on KEATS to ensure that all future teaching materials being developed and uploaded are accessible.

Ensuring all future purchases or development of digital platforms are accessible.

To improve accessibility related functionality for all users, all content uploaded to KEATS (e.g., PDFs, Microsoft Office documents and other resources) makes use of the Blackboard Ally plugin on the KEATS platform. This provides the ability to download the content in alternative formats.

We continue to promote accessibility as a core requirement for any material or system within KEATS and the complementary applications.

Ensure the KEATS interface is assessed for accessibility compliance regularly and any issues identified are added to the programme of work for resolution.
title: Accessibility Statement

text:
[Context: Feedback and contact information > What we are doing to improve accessibility]

... alternative formats.

We continue to promote accessibility as a core requirement for any material or system within KEATS and the complementary applications.

Ensure the KEATS interface is assessed for accessibility compliance regularly and any issues identified are added to the programme of work for resolution.

Ensure all tools within KEATS are WCAG compliant.

Provide guidelines on how to create accessible content.

Promote and provide feedback on the use of the KEATS accessibility tool.

#### Preparation of this accessibility statement

This statement was prepared on 20 October 2020. It was last reviewed on 23 November 2023.

This application was last tested on 30-31 October 2023 against Moodle: version 4.1.6 (release date 09/10/2023)

The test was carried out by the Education Solutions team, King's IT. Testing was carried out manually with the aid of the Siteimprove checking tool.

We tested:

* KEATS Homepage ("Dashboard"): [https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/my/](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/my/)

* KEATS staff guidance course: [https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=6293](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=6293)

* KEATS Technology for Learning - Student guides: [https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=12967](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=12967)

### 1\. Introduction

#### 1.4. The Department

#### Department facilities

The Department of Informatics has recently made the move to Bush House, a historic building with a rich history that used to be the headquarters for the BBC World Service.

All research students are provided with a desk and a high specification PC connected to the Department's network.

The Hot Desk areas are located on the 5th, 6th and 7th floor of Bush House.

For information on our Facilities & Desk Policy please visit our [Informatics SharePoint Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMSinf/SitePages/Space.aspx?web=1).

Due to shortage of lab space, students in Pending Submissions (Formally Writing Up) mode are expected to vacate their desk and clear their PC, and use a Hot Desk. If you think that due to technical requirements it is not possible for you to write up your thesis utilising the Hot Desk area, please complete the following [questionnaire](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/pluginfile.php/3066699/mod_book/chapter/137237/WUdeskandPCrequest_2018.pdf).

The labs are secured by a swipe-access system: students whose ID has not been activated for access must contact College Security and request activation.

ADDITIONAL FACILITIES:

* Shared networked printers.
* Use of the kitchen areas on 5th, 6th and 7th floor of Bush House.
* Photocopiers/scanners and general stationery: located on the 6th floor Bush House.

#### IT Support

**NMES Computing Support Service Desk
**(local software installations, local printers, local network, Departmental credentials, PCs, etc.)

Room S3.17, 3rd floor, Strand Building

Come in person for urgent issues, otherwise log a request with the [helpdesk](https://apps.nms.kcl.ac.uk/sd/).

#### **College IT support**

(KCL credentials, Student Records, email access, etc.)

Email: [8888@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:8888@kcl.ac.uk)
Telephone: 020 7848 8888
You can visit them in person at their Helpdesk at the Maughan Library, or refer to the _[College IT support internal webpage](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/it/index.aspx?src=HPIcon) _.__

You can visit them in person at their Helpdesk at the Maughan Library, or refer to the _[College IT support internal webpage](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/it/index.aspx?src=HPIcon) _.__

As stated in the College regulations (see link below), complaints are to be addressed locally in the first place (i.e. within the Department).

* If you are unhappy with any aspect of your supervision, you should first contact your second supervisor. If this does not resolve the matter, you should next contact the PhD Senior Tutor.
* If you are unhappy with a matter unrelated to your supervision, but related to the Department, you should report it to a student representative, so that it can officially be dealt by the Student-Staff Liaison Committee. You can also report any concerns to your first supervisor or to the PhD Senior Tutor.

If the issue cannot be resolved locally, students should refer to College regulations, and more specifically to the _[College complaints procedure](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/concerns-and-complaints)._
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 1\. Introduction

#### 1.5. Freedom of Speech Policy

King's College London has a statutory Code of Practice for Freedom of Speech and a related Policy Statement on Academic Freedom which sets out the values of the institution, expected conduct, and the approaches taken to securing and promoting the value of freedom of speech and academic freedom for students, staff, visiting speakers and external visitors.

When utilising this policy, care must be taken to ensure decisions and actions are consistent with the values and provisions of this Code of Practice. Where discrepancies are found, adherence to the Code of Practice and relevant statutory duties will take precedence. Any potential discrepancies should be reported to the University Secretary who will forward them to the appropriate Designated Responsible Owner for the Code of Practice.

**Keeping each other safe at King's**

We're committed to creating a welcoming, inclusive and safe environment for all members of our community. This means protecting our students, staff and visitors both on our campuses and in our digital spaces from the following unacceptable behaviours:

* bullying
* harassment
* sexual misconduct
* restriction of free speech or academic freedom

text:
### 2\. Getting Started

As a research student in the Faculty of Natural, Mathematical & Engineering Sciences, you have the guidance of experienced researchers (your supervisor and others in your research group and the whole research field) who will be able to advise you on where to start and on a line of enquiry which in their experience is likely to lead to a suitable project.

Some lines of research can prove to be too difficult, some too trivial, but most raise many interesting problems which were barely anticipated at the start and provide plenty of scope for development. As you progress and with the appropriate effort on your part, you should gain enough experience to be able to suggest your own ideas to your supervisor and perhaps develop the project along your own lines.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 2\. Getting Started

#### 2.1. Your Supervisor

All new postgraduate research students are allocated a supervisor and are invited to join their research group. You should discuss with your supervisor exactly how you should organise your research project. They will help you learn the experimental or theoretical techniques that you may need, advise on training or courses you should attend, advise you what to read and where to search to build up the relevant background knowledge of your research area, and introduce you to others working in similar areas. They may schedule regular meetings, or you may be expected to seek him/her out when you need to see them.

You will be allocated a principal supervisor and second supervisor so that you can always consult an academic staff member if, for example, your principal supervisor is at a conference or on vacation etc.

All students and supervisors are required to sign a __[Student-Supervisor Agreement](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/Student-Supervisor-Agreement.aspx)__ at the outset of the research project confirming the initial frequency of meetings and outlining how any issues in the student-supervisor relationship can be resolved. **Please note** : The Student-Supervisor Agreement is due two months after your course starts.

_It is very important to have a conversation with your supervisor about what your expectations are for the frequency and style of meetings, how information will be communicated, and what steps should be taken if either party isn't replying within the agreed upon time frame. Every student is different, every supervisor is different, and every project is different, having these conversations about expectations now will help avoid miscommunication later on._

Student-supervisor agreements should be returned to your departmental administrator or postgraduate coordinator.

If you have any further queries you should contact your supervisor.

__

**Confidentiality**

Any matter raised by you in strict confidence will not be discussed with others in a way that would endanger the principle of confidentiality. If it is recorded in writing, the document will be kept in a secure place. However, please be aware that a request for confidentiality may restrict a supervisor or other tutor's ability to help you - for example, a student who does not want others to know about their disability.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
[Context: 2\. Getting Started > 2.1. Your Supervisor]

..., the document will be kept in a secure place. However, please be aware that a request for confidentiality may restrict a supervisor or other tutor's ability to help you - for example, a student who does not want others to know about their disability.

Only if the supervisor (or other tutor) feels that there is a risk of significant harm to either you or others will thought be given to breaking the confidence. Even then, if at all possible, you will be given the chance to consent to the action and a clear explanation as to why the confidence is to be broken.

**Supervisory Excellence Awards**

These university-wide awards are an opportunity for King's research students to nominate their supervisor for a Supervisory Excellence Award. The awards provide students with the opportunity to acknowledge the support that their supervisor provides above and beyond and those who have made a difference to their education at King's. It is a student-led competition; the nominations and comments from students are the key factors in deciding each year's winners.

Students nominating their supervisor should consider the following:

* Do you have examples of when your supervisor has gone above and beyond the standard student/supervisor relationship in terms of supporting your research or your personal or professional development?
* How is their commitment to the pastoral care of supervisees?

* Are they available and approachable?

* Do they have Innovative supervisory practice(s)?

* How do they motivate you?

_Engagement with your personal and professional development which could be demonstrated through:_

* What activities you have been encouraged to participate in during your studies?

* How has your supervisor helped and encouraged your career progression?

* How has your supervisor helped and encouraged you to publish your work?

* How has your supervisor increased the quality of your research project

You will be contacted by the Doctoral College once nominations are open.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook
Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 2\. Getting Started

#### 2.3. Good Research Methods

There are a number of items of good practice it is helpful to note when you start your PhD:

* Start a laboratory notebook/logbook, even for a theoretical project. Record what you tried, why and what the outcome was, keeping a copy of the main results, including graphs, diagrams and notes. Also include lists of ideas, which you might follow up later. Writing things down can help you to clarify the problem. Make notes of your discussions with your supervisor too, and then you won't forget his/her suggestions. Record all your results and your interpretation of them, with all the parameters noted down.

* Plan your experiments (or calculations) carefully and include control or null experiments. Make sure you do not omit important conditions, parameters or regimes. Many a beautiful theory has been ruined by an inconvenient fact - and it is better that you should be the one who finds the fact.

* Spend time in the library. It is better to read throughout your project, not just at the beginning (when it can just be daunting) and the end (when you may find you have wasted time on things others have already done). You may find that others are doing similar work to yours, but although this may prevent you publishing some of your results, it may not invalidate the originality of your work for your PhD.

* Do not get worried that the first year does not seem very productive - you will learn a lot that will help you to work more productively later on.

* Ask questions all the time of your supervisor, of other students in your group and outside it, of other members of staff, and of other experts in the field.
* Join a professional society.

#### Recommended Reading

University research offers a unique opportunity to pursue a new field of study of your own choosing, free from many of the constraints that are present in the outside world. Here are some texts you might want to read at the beginning of your studies:
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 2\. Getting Started

#### 2.4. Writing your Thesis

Your thesis is a record of the research you do to qualify you for the award of the degree of PhD or MPhil. Start planning it as soon as you start your research, and if you can write up drafts of some sections as you go along, then it will be much easier to produce the final thesis in the time available. Discuss how any new ideas for research will fit into your thesis with your supervisor, so that you can avoid chasing red herrings that are not PhD material.

By the middle of your second year, you should have a good idea of what the main chapters will look like, and have some sections in an early draft. Usually, there are between 5 and 10 chapters, including an introduction and a conclusions chapter. There should be a substantial survey of the relevant literature and full explanations of the experimental or theoretical techniques used. Your main results should be presented and you should include any interpretation of them so they can be compared to other published work. The original work in a PhD thesis usually is equivalent to two or more published papers.

For further details please visit _[Doctoral College Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/KPRH/SitePages/Format-of-thesis-and-binding.aspx)_ & _[PhD etheses](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/researchsupport/open-access/phd-etheses)_
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 3\. Training & Transferable Skills

During your PhD you should undertake training, your research project work itself constitutes a major training component but in line with Research Council requirements and QAA recommendations, you should also acquire generic skills and skills in research methods.

The university recommends that all research students should undertake up to **10 days of training and development activities per year of your studies**(5 for PT students) this can include; presenting at conferences or research seminars and running events as well as more traditional training courses.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 3\. Training & Transferable Skills

#### 3.1. What Counts

Skills development is an ongoing and positive process that should continue throughout your career, either in academia or elsewhere. A significant opportunity of the wide range of available courses helping you to acquire valuable skills is that you may have an advantage over other candidates when you are applying for jobs.

The most important consideration for whether an activity will be considered appropriate is that it has a **clearly identifiable developmental component.** You should discuss this with your supervisor when you are completing your monitoring report form and apply this basic rule of thumb.

Courses that involve skills specific to the student's project and are of no use beyond the end of the PhD are **not** considered to be 'transferable' skills training. However, if the course provides the student with skills that would be useful in their later career than this would count.

To be eligible, a course or other training should focus on **non-essential skills and activities** that can be applied both during and beyond the student's course of study. Any course or event that you attend which provides added value to your studies or your personal/professional development will be considered eligible.

For example, courses such as 'masterclass in public speaking', 'networking', 'CVs and applications', 'creativity and problem solving', 'negotiating', 'small group teaching', 'project/staff management', 'writing effective research proposals', 'time management' etc are considered transferable skills as they can easily translate to a range of future jobs.

**Conferences and presentations** : Attending a conference for the whole day **does not count** , just the time taken to give a poster or platform presentation and the time spent getting feedback on it (usually a maximum of half a day).

The time spent preparing slides or a poster for a presentation is considered part of normal student activities. However, **practicing the presentation and getting feedback from supervisors and colleagues** has a clear training component and is therefore eligible. Equally, for a poster presentation: **revising the poster** after obtaining comments from supervisor and colleagues and giving a **mock poster presentation** are valid activities. Attending **skills-based workshops** at a conference would count.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
[Context: 3\. Training & Transferable Skills > 3.1. What Counts]

... therefore eligible. Equally, for a poster presentation: **revising the poster** after obtaining comments from supervisor and colleagues and giving a **mock poster presentation** are valid activities. Attending **skills-based workshops** at a conference would count.

Exclusions would also include essential activities that are required for you to complete your studies or would normally be undertaken as part of your project. For example: inductions; training for the Home Office to obtain a personal license under the Scientific Procedures (Animals) Act 1986; courses on phlebotomy or how to take a clinical history; taking part on rotas for lab cleaning or tissue culture maintenance; general ordering of lab supplies. A statistics course that covers essential methods required for your project would **not** count, but one that expands and improves your understanding of similar methods would be eligible.

Please remember that it is **your** **responsibility** to discuss your training activities with your supervisor at least once a year and that you need to record all of your development activities on your progress monitoring reports.

* The Research Councils require all of their funded students and post-docs to fulfil the training requirement of ten days per year, and the University has endorsed the policy that all research students should be encouraged to attend this amount of transferable skills training. Some Faculties monitor this through their progress reporting system. Please contact your supervisor or your Faculty for further detail.

More information can be found on the [NMES Graduate School Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/PGR-Training-%26-Events.aspx).
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 3\. Training & Transferable Skills

#### 3.2. Faculty & University Opportunities

Both the Faculty and the university offer an extensive range of opportunities to allow you to develop a wide variety of skills to help further enhance your employment prospects.

These courses allow you to hone your research skills, along with other [transferable skills](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/study-legacy/doctoral-studies/doctoral-training). These skills include project management, communication and entrepreneurial training. In NMES, there are [opportunities to teach](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/KPRH/SitePages/Doctoral_Teaching.aspx), as well as other relevant training opportunities.

Here you will find information and further links to help you understand what is available to you from both the Faculty, the university and elsewhere.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 3\. Training & Transferable Skills

#### 3.3. Training Needs Analysis

Supervisors are required to formally record the training each student completes. At the start of each year of study a Training Needs Analysis must be carried out by supervisors with their students. The student and supervisor will need to identify skills the student needs to improve or acquire and use this as a basis for devising a training plan for the coming year.

As stated in the [Core Code of Practice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/assets/policyzone/teaching/pgt-code-of-practice.pdf) (section 7.3), '_It is recognised that the research project work itself constitutes the major training component of the study programme, but in line with Research Council requirements and QAA recommendations, students should also demonstrate that they are acquiring generic skills and skills in research methods_.'

When starting your training needs analysis you should:

You may want to consider the following areas:

* Knowledge and intellectual abilities - Learning a language, IT training, Doctoral essentials training
* Personal effectiveness - Time management, managing stress, interview skills
* Research governance and organisation - Effective research, research ethics
* Engagement, influence and impact - Conference attendance, presentation skills drafting grant proposal

Consider the best way to acquire practical and theoretical knowledge for each skill area e.g. a training course, visiting another institution etc.

Once you have done this you should meet to discuss your thoughts and suggestions with your supervisor and devise a training plan for the coming year, which can be added to or modified throughout the year.

Full-time students should undertake the equivalent of ten days of training and development activities per year of study.

Part-time students must undertake a minimum of five days/sessions per year. Development activities should be tailored to the student's requirements and do not have to be limited to five sessions only.

To learn more about Student Train & Events please visit the [NMES Graduate School Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/PGR-Training-%26-Events.aspx).
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 3\. Training & Transferable Skills

#### 3.4. Doctoral Development Programme

The [Doctoral Development Programme](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/KPRH/SitePages/Key_Training_Themes.aspx) offers a range of live and on-demand training opportunities on various fundamental topics which aim to support you in becoming a well-rounded and effective researcher.

The skills you develop during your doctorate will be highly transferable and will help you to excel during your doctorate whilst also boosting your career prospects in academia, industry, and beyond. You will develop naturally as you carry out your research and complete your doctorate, but by attending workshops, engaging with support services, and accessing on-demand resources you can gain insights from experts, enabling you to develop your skills effectively.

###### Modes of Delivery

__

The Doctoral College's provision is delivered as both live interactive workshops and on-demand resources, complemented by 1-2-1 support, so you can choose the format that suits you best.

* **Live interactive workshops** : live workshops can offer a great opportunity to connect with other researchers. They aim to provide a stimulating and inclusive environment for everyone who attends our events, and to remove barriers to participation and engagement wherever they can.

* **On-demand resources** : With on-demand training, you can choose your own learning path - whether that is working through a course in order, or dipping in and out of modules.

Find out more about the live workshops, on-demand options, and other development opportunities the Doctoral College offer by exploring their development themes below. You can find out more about accessing their workshop booking portal and on-demand resources on [this page](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/KPRH/SitePages/How-to-access-our-development-opportunities.aspx).
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 3\. Training & Transferable Skills

#### 3.5. Professional Skills for a Globalised World

[Professional Skills for a Globalised World](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/search.php?search=Professional+Skills+for+a+Globalised+World) is an online module for all students in Informatics.**** This course has been developed specifically for our students, in collaboration with a student advisory board, and aims to provide you with the skills you will need to work effectively with people from different backgrounds and with different perspectives. The course is interactive and includes relevant scenarios developed together with the student advisory board.

The course should only take around one hour to complete and all students - Undergraduate, Masters and PhD students - should complete this course by the end of the first semester. Students who are new to King's will be enrolled on the course on KEATS during the first few weeks of term and will receive an email once this has happened with further instructions.

After completing the course you will receive a certificate of completion and be able to list the training on your CV. The skills you will develop by taking this course are highly valued by employers. As stated by Google: "Only by actively engaging different perspectives can we challenge and stretch our thinking, enrich the experiences of our employees, and empower every person and every organization on the planet to achieve more." This course will help you develop the skills you need to contribute to this aim.

Nearly 2,000 students took the course last year, and 82% of them said that they would recommend it to another student. When we asked last year's students to tell us about the most useful thing they took away from the course, responses clustered around several themes, which are exemplified in the following quotes:

* "[the course has] given me knowledge on how to work with people with different backgrounds and perspectives and why it's crucial within a workplace"
* "The importance of fighting against implicit biases. This course has made me even more aware the innate mistakes we make when approaching how we work and communicate with others, however, thanks to this course, I know what to do to make the environment I'm in, the best for everyone around to feel included and supported
* "I found learning how to report and react to certain situations and events happening, such as a fellow classmate being bullied or harassed, the most useful."
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 4\. Key Stages & Assessment Requirements

Full-time study, supported by the Research Councils and other bodies, is normally for a period of three years (six years part-time), after which a further (usually non-supported) year is allowed. After the three years (or part-time equivalent), full-time or part-time students can choose to re-enrol for one final fourth year (full tuition fees), during which they are allowed to transfer to Writing Up (reduced fees) or submit their thesis. Students, however, will not be able to exceed this time-limit.

Below are the main points of the progression requirements for PhD students in Informatics Department. For more detailed information, please see the [PGR Researcher Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/PGR-Researcher-Hub.aspx).

* Within three months from the start of their programme, students are required to complete a Student-Supervisor Agreement form.

* At the beginning of their programme, students are also required to develop a training plan (Training Needs Analysis) in agreement with their supervisor.

* Subject to satisfactory performance, assessed by means of Individual Progress Review (IPR) reports, students are permitted to progress from one year of study to the next for the first three years (six years part-time).

* By nine months of registration (18 months part-time), students' progress is assessed on the basis of a technical report and viva. The positive outcome of this 9-month progress assessment allows the students to progress to the second year of their programme.

* All students entering our PhD programmes at King's are initially enrolled with the MPhil registration. Within 9 to 18 months of full time registration, students are assessed (by report and viva) for the upgrade from the MPhil to PhD registration. The final decisions whether the students progress to the PhD registration have to be made by the end of 18 months of full time registration. To meet this deadline, the students are expected to complete their first attempt at upgrade by the end of Month 15. This will allow for the second attempt, if needed, to be completed by the end of Month 18. (The students who are successful on the first attempt are immediately transferred to the PhD registration without need for any further upgrade assessments.)
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

* On successful completion of three (six part-time) years of study, students can transfer to Pending Submission (Formally Writing Up) status, which is one calendar year for both full-time and part-time students.

text:
### 4\. Key Stages & Assessment Requirements

#### 4.1. Student-Supervisor Agreement

The Student-Supervisor Agreement is one of the first things you will do in collaboration with your supervisor in the first few weeks after you enroll on your course. It is your chance to agree on the format and frequency of your meetings with your supervisor, and to finalise your research title and topic.

It is also where you will confirm who will be acting as your second supervisor. This is an important role in supporting you through your time studying in the Faculty. It is the responsibility of your supervisor to arrange the recruitment of your second supervisor.

This form can be brought to your first meeting with your supervisor and used to set out your respective expectations and agreements for supervision.

All students and supervisors are required to sign a [Student-Supervisor Agreement](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/pluginfile.php/12113081/mod_book/chapter/866178/NMES%20Student-Supervisor-Agreement.docx) at the outset of the research project confirming the initial frequency of meetings and outlining how any issues in the student-supervisor relationship can be resolved.

* The completed and signed Agreement is to be submitted to the [Senior PGR Officer](mailto:informatics-pgr@kcl.ac.uk) within **one month from registration.**

For more info please visit [NMES Graduate School Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/Student-Supervisor-Agreement.aspx)
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 4\. Key Stages & Assessment Requirements

#### 4.2. Nine-/Eighteen-Month Review

By the 9-month point (or 18-month part-time equivalent), students are expected to complete a formal progress review consisting of the following components:

#### Written Report

__

Students must submit a summary report (maximum **10 pages**) to their supervisors and an independent assessor.

#### Viva

__

Students must also complete a short viva, presenting their submission to the Review Panel as part of an oral examination. The format of the viva is flexible and should be discussed with the supervisor, as there is no fixed structure.

The topic of the report and viva should be agreed with the supervisor in advance and may include **one or more** of the following:

* A summary of the literature survey

* A project specification

* A report on a completed piece of work

If a student has made exceptional progress (for example, by submitting a journal or conference paper), this work may be used as part of - or, where appropriate, all of - the review submission.

* * *

### Review Panel

__

The review will be conducted by a panel consisting of:

* The first and second supervisors

* An independent assessor (who is not part of the supervisory team)

* * *

### Purpose of the Review

__

The purpose of the 9-Month Progress Review is to confirm that continuing with the programme is the right course of action for both the student and the institution (Department, Faculty, and College).

Please note that this is a **department-level milestone** and ** _does not_ constitute the formal Upgrade review**.

* * *

### Submitting the Review Form

__

Following the review, the panel must complete and sign the attached **'[9-Month Progress Review](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/pluginfile.php/12113081/mod_book/chapter/866179/9%20Month%20Review%20Form.doc)' **or**'[18-month Progress Review](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/pluginfile.php/12113081/mod_book/chapter/866179/18%20Month%20Review%20Form.doc)'** form and return it to [**informatics-pgr@kcl.ac.uk**](mailto:informatics-pgr@kcl.ac.uk)**** within **one week after** the 9-month deadline.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
[Context: Submitting the Review Form]

...%20Review%20Form.doc)'** form and return it to [**informatics-pgr@kcl.ac.uk**](mailto:informatics-pgr@kcl.ac.uk)**** within **one week after** the 9-month deadline.

This review is an important milestone within Informatics, providing evidence that the student is making satisfactory progress and that the Department and Faculty are justified in recommending continuation on the programme.

* * *

### Unsatisfactory Progress

__

If the panel determines that progress is unsatisfactory, remedial steps must be agreed, and a second formal review must take place before the end of the student's first 12 months. In such cases, a new review form will be required to record the updated outcome.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 4\. Key Stages & Assessment Requirements

#### 4.3. Individual Progress Reports

Individual Progress Reports monitor your study and development throughout your study. You will set progress objectives with your supervisors to have a structured timeline of how you will hit milestones-for an idea of these milestones visit [here](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/Individ.aspx). It is, also, a crucial time to identify if there are any issues affecting your study.

Individual Progress Reports are part of the regular online monitoring process carried on by the Faculty. They are to be completed by student and supervisor, with the outcome confirmed by the Senior PGR Tutor.

For further information please refer to the [NMES Graduate School Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/PGR-Researcher-Hub.aspx).
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 4\. Key Stages & Assessment Requirements

#### 4.4. MPhil to PhD Upgrade

Research students at King's are normally registered as MPhil students. To receive a PhD they must upgrade to PhD status by undergoing a formal review. This process will begin after 9 months of study and should be completed within 18 months (or the part-time equivalent). Please note: the general expectation is that the upgrade is completed within 12 months of study, but 18 months is the final deadline (in cases such as students needing more time or need to take the exam for a second time). The upgrade will involve the submission of a written report and a formal viva where the student will present their report and answer questions. Further information about the the upgrade, can be found on [NMES Graduate School Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/Upgrading-from-MPhil-to-PhD.aspx).

##### Upgrade Process

The upgrade process is completed online via Student Records. This is a four stage process:

Stage 1: The supervisor will initiate the online process to record panel members and set the viva date

Stage 2: The student will be required to upload documentation and make any comments

Stage 3: Document circulation and viva

Stage 4: Recording of upgrade outcome

_The following outcomes are possible:_

* _Upgrade attempt successful_ \- No further action required.

* _Refer for further review_

* _Minor amendments_ \- The Panel chair will confirm deadline for amendments to be completed. Online system may be used to monitor this or can be completed outside of the system.

* _Repeat full upgrade process_ \- The Panel chair will confirm new deadline for second attempt.

* _Upgrade attempt unsuccessful_ \- Remain on MPhil or initiate discussions to terminate study.

**Please note** that the 9 month review is a separate progress check. Please see [9th/18th Month Review](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/Individ.aspx) for more details. The emails students and supervisors will receive from Registry Services at 9 months will be for the upgrade.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 4\. Key Stages & Assessment Requirements

#### 4.5. Transfer to Pending Submission (Previously Writing Up) Status

When a student has completed the data collection and research required for the PhD, they may be transferred from full or part-time student status to 'writing-up' according to the procedure established by the Faculty of Registration. Transferring to writing-up is not an automatic right.

**Please note** that the cost of transfer to 'writing-up' status currently stands at a total of £350.

The maximum period permitted for writing-up for both full and part-time students is**one year**. Students are not allowed to continue on the writing-up status beyond this period. If a student does not submit within the one year allowed for writing-up but is permitted to continue their degree, they are transferred back to full or part-time status and **charged** the appropriate fee.

For further Information about the transfer, as well as the required form, please visit the [NMES Graduate School Hub](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/Writing-U.aspx).

**Please note** that the completed and signed Transfer form is to be submitted to the PGR Officer, who will have it countersigned by the Senior Research Tutor and forward it to Registry Services. A copy of the form will be retained in the Departmental student file.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 5\. Plagiarism & other forms of cheating

Plagiarism is a form of cheating and a serious academic offence. All allegations of plagiarism and cheating will be investigated and may result in action being taken under the College's [misconduct regulations](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academic-misconduct-policy).

It is important that, as a student, you understand that all types of cheating are unacceptable and will not assist your learning in the long term. You should also be aware that academic staff have considerable expertise in identifying plagiarism and have access to electronic detection services to assist them.

Note also that credit can only be given once for a particular piece of assessed work. Submitting the same piece of work (or a significant part thereof) twice for assessment will be regarded as cheating, even when the work was submitted as part of a different programme or at a different institution.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 6\. Policies & Support

#### 6.1. Attendance & Absence for Research Students

Although the Department allows for some flexibility, you are expected to regularly attend College and make use of your desk and PC. If you are absent for more than one or two days, due to illness or because you are travelling to a conference or Summer School, the Programmes Administrator must also be informed by email.

You must maintain regular contact with your supervisor and make satisfactory progress, as judged by your supervisor, the Senior Research Tutor and the Head of Department.

It is expected that full-time students will undertake a full working week (16 hours per week for part-time students) on their MPhil/PhD. You may be required to attend specified courses and all students are encouraged to attend relevant transferable skills training.

#### Holidays

Students are entitled to annual leave of 40 working days per year (pro-rata for part-time students), inclusive of public holidays and university closure dates. Students must agree the process for notifying of intended annual leave with their supervisors, and students should notify their supervisors well in advance of any leave starting. Excessive absences should be reported to the faculty via normal progress report procedures.

_Students receiving industrial sponsorship are expected to bear in mind their obligations to the sponsor in planning their leave._

Holiday dates must be agreed with the first supervisor.

#### Off-Campus Study Arrangements

_The Department must be notified of such arrangements and the relevant request form is to be submitted to the PGR Officer._

Students are permitted to spend part of their programme 'off-campus' under conditions prescribed by the faculty of registration and within the framework of the Academic Regulations for Research degrees, as noted in the [Remote study guidance](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/Shared%20Documents/Forms/AllItems.aspx?id=%2Fsites%2FNMESGS%2FShared%20Documents%2FNMES%20PGR%20Remote%20Study%20Guidance%2Epdf&parent=%2Fsites%2FNMESGS%2FShared%20Documents).

The Off-campus study form should only be completed for students who are expecting to spend periods of time off campus for the purpose of research (e.g. data collection, research projects, joint PhD programmes). Students must agree detailed arrangements with their supervisor and inform the faculty's registry office by completing and submitting the form every time they plan to be off campus.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

In this case, please reach out the visa team, check [here](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/campuslife/services/student-advice-support/how/International-Student-Support), for advice on this matter. If there are no issues of violation, then we will seek departmental and supervisors' approval for the request. **Please note that academic fees remain payable to King's College London at a non-reduced rate for students completing off-campus study.**

text:
### 6\. Policies & Support

#### 6.2. What to do if things go wrong

If you feel that there is an important issue that needs to be addressed, this can usually be dealt with informally by discussing with your supervisor or other members of staff. We are always happy to listen to student concerns and to do what we can to find constructive ways forward. We try to ensure there are routes for you to solve problems. For example, you should contact the person identified below for the relevant problem areas.

* If you are unhappy _with any aspect_ of your supervision, you should try to resolve this directly with your supervisor. You can also speak to your second supervisor or the Research Senior Tutor. The key contacts at King's are outlined in [section 1.3](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665209&chapterid=866162) of the handbook.

* If you are unhappy with a matter _unrelated_ to your supervision, but related to your PhD programme or to the department, you should report it to a PhD student representative so that it can be considered by the [departmental MPhil/PhD Staff-Student Liaison Committee](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665209&chapterid=866175).

* If you feel that your particular concern _has still_ not been addressed, or if there is a different concern that you wish to bring to our attention, it is important that you contact the Research Senior Tutor, who can then take appropriate action, or advise you about what to do next.

* Finally, if you still feel that your concern has not been addressed, you can contact the Head of Department, but you should only do this, if you have already tried to resolve the matter via the steps above.

We believe that this should address the vast majority of problems that might arise. However, if problems remain, you can follow the college's formal complaints procedure, which is explained at [Concerns and Complaints](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/concerns-and-complaints).
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

### 6\. Policies & Support

#### 6.4. Supporting Disabled Students

King's College London has a range of services and support to help disabled students throughout their duration of study. The Disability Support & Inclusion Team (DST), specifically, provides information, guidance, and advice to current disabled students. The Equality Act of 2010 defines a disability as 'having a physical or mental impairment that has a 'substantial' and 'long-term' negative effect on your ability to do normal daily activities'. Moreover, the DST mentions that King's College London include long-term medical or mental health conditions and Specific learning difficulties (SpLDs) in defining 'disability'.

Additionally, the DST will support students in creating a King's Inclusion Plan (KIP), provide support when students are applying for [funding](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01119/en-us) such as Disabled Student Allowance, additional learning support, and signposting to other support services across (and external to) King's. The team will advise, via the KIP where a Personalised Emergency Evacuation Plan (PEEP) or [Personalised Assessment arrangements (PAA)](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01602/en-us) are needed. Details of what support is available can be found here: [Student Services Online](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01639/en-us).

Resources:

* [King's College London Disability Support and Inclusion](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/disability)
* [King's College London Disability Support and Inclusion (Intranet)](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/student/SupportandAdvice/das/index)

### 6\. Policies & Support

#### 6.6. Free Mental Health Apps

#### Stress & Anxiety

App | Cost | Description & Features Sanvello | Free (iOS&Android) | A straightforward app that enables users to tracktheir mood and health behaviours (sleep, diet, alcohol) over time, set daily goals, record thoughts, access relaxation exercises and connect with others in groups centred around different problems. Some CBT techniques. Recommend for people experiencing mild - moderate stress, anxiety or low mood. Recommend to use as an adjunct to having CBT or for self-management during and post therapy.

#### Worry Management

App | Cost | Description & Features ReachOutWorry Time | Free (iOS&Android) | A user-friendly app for people who are experiencing difficulties with worry. It enables users to try to reduce disruptive worries. Provides self-help exercises to help users schedule a time to worry, to improve awareness of thought patterns and let go of unhelpful worries. App uses CBT-based techniques. Recommend to use as an adjunct to having CBT or for self-management during and post therapy.

#### Depression & Low Mood

App | Cost | Description & Features Catch It | Free (iOS&Android) | A user-friendly CBT-based app for people experiencing mild to moderate depression. Enables users to learn how to notice feelings, rate emotions, monitor and challenge thoughts. Emphasis is on thought challenging techniques. App has a personal pin code for users. Recommend as useful to complement therapy at start of CBT, and for self-management during and post therapy.

### Sleep

App | Cost | Description & Features CBT-i | Free (iOS&Android) | A CBT-based app designed to help users to make practical changes to manage and improve sleep. Provides psycho-education on sleep, a sleep diary to monitor sleep patterns, how to use sleep restriction, as well as relaxation and thought balancing techniques. Recommend for standalone use supported by a therapist, or self-management during and post CBT work for insomnia.

### Alcohol Management

App | Cost | Description & Features DrinkAware | Free (iOS&Android) | A helpful app that enables users to set goals and make practical changes around alcohol consumption. It has an alcohol diary where users can track alcohol use and calculate money spent on alcohol. Users can set goals and learn about risks associated with heavy drinking and health benefits of reducing alcohol use. Suitable for ages 18+.

### Mindfulness & Relaxation

Advanced exercises require subscription fee. Sessions fit into everyday life, and app offers reminders and a personal progress page. Recommend for anyone experiencing mild to moderate stress, anxiety or low mood.

App | Cost | Description & Features Calm | Free(iOS&Android) | A user-friendly app that provides guided audio meditation, relaxation and mindfulness tracks and exercises. App opens with view of sea and sound of waves. Suitable for people experiencing stress or mild anxiety or low mood.

### Eating Disorders

App | Cost | Description & Features Recovery Record | Free(iOS&Android) | A CBT-based app for eating disorders. Enables users to record meals, thoughts & feelings; create customisable meal plans and personal goals. Can track progress through questionnaires. Has as a virtual rewards system, gives personal reminders, and user can link up to a clinician and community that can offer real-time feedback and support. Recommend for use during therapy and self-management post-therapy. Knowledge of CBT helpful for using app.

### Suicide Prevention

App | Cost | Description & Features Stay Alive | Free (iOS&Android) | Helpful and informative app that offers help and support to people who are feeling suicidal and to people concerned about someone else. Enables users to create a safety plan. Includes information and strategies for staying safe. A 'LifeBox' to which the user can upload photos from their phone reminding them of their reasons to stay alive. Provides links to other support services and a location-enabled function to show the user support services in their vicinity. Recommend for use supported by a therapist or GP, or self-management.

### 7\. Funding Opportunities & Financial Support

#### 7.1. Research, Training and Support Grant (RTSG)

**VIDEO:**[RTSG & Travel Guidance Session - Thursday 6th November 2025](https://emckclac-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/k2371480_kcl_ac_uk/EfPAc6D8CjxCusizJaV5GyoBS-ErWew0cykBkIcIPZhddA?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=ZPtnHR) \- Hari Indran (Business Operations Officer) hosted a detailed RTSG & Travel Guidance Session

###### Research, Training and Support Grant (RTSG)

All students have an allocation of money to put towards travel costs and consumables directly related to their research during their PhD programme. This allocation is known as the Research Training Support Grant (RTSG). The amount and duration of your RTSG funding depends on your specific funder.

For RTSG guidance, please refer to the [NMES Graduate School](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/RTSG-%26-Travelling-on-Student-Business.aspx).

For students outside their funded period, please consult the pages on the [King's Doctoral College Conference Fund](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/KPRH/SitePages/Conference-Fund.aspx), which takes applications in different competition rounds throughout the year.

**Please note:** If you would need extra guidance on the below steps, then please reach out to **Hari Indran** at [informatics-business@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:informatics-business@kcl.ac.uk). He will be happy to answer any questions you have!

**How can I claim RTSG?**

2. **For Overseas Travel & UK Fieldwork only **(i.e. not for UK conference travel or other expenses),**** please obtain email confirmation from your supervisor that they approve of the travel taking place. You do not need to obtain quotes at this stage, but you should have a clear travel purpose and dates. You will need to attach this email approval to both your business travel authorisation and RTSG approval forms.
3. **For any overseas travel or UK fieldwork** *, students must complete the [Business Travel Authorisation Form](https://kcl-dwp.onbmc.com/dwp/app/#/itemprofile/6204) via helix, covering your sustainability and travel risk assessment. Please complete the **student form**(not staff form), for RTSG travel spend and **attach your supervisor approval**. The form must be completed each time you travel, but you can resubmit a previously completed form if travelling regularly to the same destination.

Please note that the Business Travel Authorisation Form & the Insurance application do **not** have to be completed for UK travel, only for UK fieldwork trips and for travel overseas. However, for UK travel you are encouraged to have an informal discussion with your supervisor regarding risk assessment considerations for your trip:

- any potential risks.

- consideration of any mitigating actions.

- consideration of any public health advice, as necessary.

- escalation process in case of emergencies.

* **London Travel:** Travel costs, accommodation or subsistence within London is not reimbursed. If some travel costs are incurred in excess of normal home to work travel, this may be reimbursed but check with your Business Operations Teams first.

* **Accommodation Type:** Only hotel stays are reimbursed.**Airbnb, apartments and other private rental accommodation will not reimbursed without express approval from the University's Travel Manager**([judit.korosztos@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:judit.korosztos@kcl.ac.uk)) . The University recognises that research in some countries, especially Low and Middle Income Countries, necessitates more flexibility when it comes to accommodation bookings. Travellers must demonstrate that:****

- 12.4.1. They contacted the University's Travel Manager before booking and he/she was satisfied that there was no reasonable alternative in the circumstances;

- and 12.4.2. The outcome of a risk assessment demonstrated that any risk could be mitigated and managed. Key Travel offers accredited serviced apartments for stays up to 28 nights.

Cost reasons alone do not justify the selection of a private rental accommodation as the University has a legal duty to ensure the safety and wellbeing of its Travellers when undertaking University Business. Please see the [Sustainable Travel Policy](https://chrome-extension/efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https:/www.kcl.ac.uk/assets/policyzone/finance/sustainable-travel-policy.pdf) for full information.

* **Car Hire:** [Car hire](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/procurement/travel-services-and-events/vhire) is now accessible through Key Travel, with payments invoiced directly to King's, similar to other travel services, though, travel by road is not recommended.****

* **[King's new sustainability polic](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/procurement/travel-services-and-events/sustainable-travel-policy-q-a)y** mandates that mainland UK travel occur by train, and discourages air travel for international destinations within 5 hours by train.****

* **Effective April 2024,** alcohol costs will no longer be eligible for reimbursement through the Research Training Support Grant (RTSG), including as part of a meal at a conference. This change is in line with updated funder guidelines and applies to **all PGR students** in NMES, regardless of funding type, including self-funded students with RTSG accounts.

For additional information, please visit the [Expense Payments page](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/finance/ap/ExpensePayments) on the King's College London Intranet.

****

**What can my RTSG Funding be used for?**

RTSG funds can be used for the following costs incurred as part of your research:

* Research Consumables
* Research Equipment
* Travel (Standard class or Economy only)
* Conferences
* Internships
* Workshops
* Short course attendance

**RTSG Flowchart**

****

**What can't my RTSG Funding be used for?**

RTSG funds cannot be used for the following:

* Resources for a student prior to the start date of their scholarship
* Events occurring after the end date of a student's scholarship
* Capital equipment or facilities which the University would normally be expected to provide
* Equipment provided as standard or generally available through the department e.g. stationery
* Expenses relating to supervisor visits (to or from the student) during fieldwork/offsite placements of less than nine months
* Training that all departmental postgraduate students are required to attend, for example health and safety courses
* Books and other reading materials available through libraries
* Publication Fees
* Basic consumables common to the lab

**Alcohol Costs**

Effective April 2024, alcohol costs will no longer be eligible for reimbursement through the Research Training Support Grant (RTSG), including as part of a meal at a conference. This change is in line with updated funder guidelines and applies to all PGR students in NMES, regardless of funding type, including self-funded students with RTSG accounts.

For further guidance, please consider checking out '[RTSG & Travelling on Student Business](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/RTSG-%26-Travelling-on-Student-Business.aspx "RTSG & Travelling on Student Business")' Hubpage on the NMES Graduate School.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 7\. Funding Opportunities & Financial Support

#### 7.2. Key Travel Information

Students can get a quote from our travel agent [Key Travel](https://my.keytravel.com/uk/log-in?RedirectTo=/uk/my-travel/booking-online "https://my.keytravel.com/uk/log-in?RedirectTo=/uk/my-travel/booking-online") and King's can then pay directly, to get the quote you need to be set up as a _booker_. You will be able to book the flight/hotel/Eurostar from Key Travel, but you may still need to submit a student expenses claim for meals and local travel.

**Setting up an account on Key Travel**

Email [bookers@keytravel.com](mailto:bookers@keytravel.com) stating the following.

1. Your full name:
2. Email address:
3. Phone number:
4. Organisation: King's College London
5. Department: King's College London - NMES - Informatics

**Step-by-Step Guide:****Key Travel - How to Make a Booking**

**Step 1: Create a Traveler Profile**

1. After receiving a confirmation that you booker account has been set up, please to the [online catalogue](https://my.keytravel.com/uk/log-in?RedirectTo=/uk/my-travel/booking-online).
2. Create a traveler profile to enter personal and passport-related data.

**Step 2: Select Flights or Hotels**

1. Choose flights or hotels and add them to your basket.
2. Refer to the [Detailed Guide](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/procurement/resources/key-travel-online-booking-tool-user-guide.pdf) with visual instructions or the [Simple Guide](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/procurement/resources/key-travel-simple-booking-guide-kcl-may22.pdf) for assistance.

**Important Notes:**

* Only book economy class flights.
* Bookings may not be refundable; proceed with caution.
* Hotel accommodation is capped using the HMRC "room rate" per night (see the Expenses policy site for details).

**Step 3: Share the Quote**
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
[Context: 7\. Funding Opportunities & Financial Support > 7.2. Key Travel Information]

... economy class flights.
* Bookings may not be refundable; proceed with caution.
* Hotel accommodation is capped using the HMRC "room rate" per night (see the Expenses policy site for details).

**Step 3: Share the Quote**

1. Once items are added to your basket, share the quote with [informatics-business@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:informatics-business@kcl.ac.uk).
2. Follow the [instructions](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/procurement/resources/sharing-your-trip-with-a-booker-in-the-online-booking-tool.pdf) for sharing the quote.

**Step 4: Raise a Purchase Order (PO)**

1. A purchase order is raised for the value of the quote.
2. A PO number is generated after the PO is approved (approval may take 2-3 days).

**Quick Tip:**

* If the combined cost of flights and hotel exceeds £1,000, send two separate quotes (one for flights, one for the hotel) for faster approval.

**Step 5: Confirm the Booking**

1. After receiving the PO number, press the "Book" button.
2. During checkout, enter the PO number.
3. Once checkout is complete, the quote becomes a booking.
4. Key Travel will email you all relevant details.

**Additional Information: Adding Baggage for Flights**

* When raising a quote, baggage options may be presented (usually for long-haul flights).
* Check the number of check-in bags included.
* Hand luggage may be included by default per airline's standard luggage allowance.
* For budget airlines (e.g., EasyJet), baggage may not be included in the quote.

**If baggage is needed:**

* Add baggage at the final stage of booking (when entering the PO number).
* Be aware this incurs an additional cost not covered in the initial quote and PO.
* Inform at the quote stage if baggage is needed to include the cost in the PO.
* Alternatively, purchase baggage through the airline's website or at the airport check-in.

**Note:** Always check the airline's baggage policy.

For more information please visit the [Travel Services](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/procurement/travel-services-and-events/travel) & [Expense Policy](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/finance/ap/expenses/expense-policy).
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 7\. Funding Opportunities & Financial Support

#### 7.3. Travel Insurance

If you will be going on any trips abroad as part of your studies, such as conferences etc., you can apply for travel insurance through the College.

**Please note that Research Students are not automatically covered.**
To obtain standard insurance coverage students must complete a Travel Application form for **each** trip abroad.

The policy is a standard travel policy; it covers King's research students travelling outside the UK in connection with College studies. The standard policy only includes brief incidental local sightseeing as part of the trip and does not cover private holiday travel activities. For further information please go to the College Travel Insurance internal webpage (see linked image on the right-hand side).

To apply for the College standard travel insurance policy:

* Complete the Travel Application form you can find at the bottom of the _[College Travel Insurance web page](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/finance/treasry/insure.aspx "College Travel Insurance web page")_
* Have the form signed by your first supervisor
* Return the form the the Programmes Administrator.

The Programmes Administrator will have the form countersigned by the Head of Department and scan it through to the relevant office.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 7\. Funding Opportunities & Financial Support

#### 7.4. PGR Carers Support Scheme

The _[NMES PGR Student Carer's support scheme](https://emckclac.sharepoint.com/sites/NMESGS/SitePages/PGR-Carers-Support-Scheme.aspx)_ is designed to support Postgraduate Research (PGR) Students who may be parents or carers with additional care costs incurred as a result of attending commitments which fall outside of their normal working hours (which may vary between people and circumstances), such as conferences and networking events related to their PhD studies.

Additionally, there is support available from the King's College London Student Union (KCLSU) for student parents-which can be accessed [here](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/campuslife/services/student-advice-support/how/parents/student-parents).
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 8\. PGR Student Voice

As a PGR Student in the NMES faculty, it is important that your time at Kings is positive, enriching and as stress-free as possible. A way King's works to ensure that this is a reality for all students is through providing forums and opportunities for the student voice to be heard.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 8\. PGR Student Voice

#### 8.1. Student-Staff Liaison Committee (SSLC)

The Postgraduate Research Student-Staff Liaison Committee is a group consisting of PGR student representatives from each year, and PGR Academic and Professional Services Staff from the Informatics Department.

This Committee meets up **three times** each Academic year. The purpose of this Committee is to discuss and feedback on any matters about PGR Students, examples include (mind this is not exhaustive), PGR Student facilities and resources, the PGR programme, their experience and enrichment, and future suggestions.

Any feedback arising from these meetings will then be presented to the Faculty and the Department through the PGR Oversight Committee.

If you would like to offer feedback on aspects of the PGR Experience, please, feel free to reach out to any of the Committee members. This will be raised at the next Committee meeting.

##### Committee Chair

__

* Dr. Oana Cocarascu - PGR Senior Tutor

##### Committee Secretary

__

* Katie Deeble - Senior PGR Officer
