text:
### 8\. PGR Student Voice

#### 8.2. Postgraduate Research Experience Survey (PRES)

[PRES](https://www.advance-he.ac.uk/reports-publications-and-resources/postgraduate-research-experience-survey-pres) is a national online survey run biennially by the Higher Education Academy in conjunction with individual HE institutions. The survey is designed to provide participating institutions with feedback on:

* What their postgraduate research students consider are important factors in determining their satisfaction with their educational experience

* Their actual levels of satisfaction

* Benchmarking against comparable institutions

The departments in the Faculty of Natural and Mathematics Science all make every effort to consult their students as much as possible during the course of their degree. They have active and open Staff Student Liaison Committees and also take the specific module feedback given very seriously. Your departmental web pages will give details, or you can contact your departmental office for more information.

We really want to know what your concerns are - tell us, and we will try to make your experience in the College better. Similarly, let us know what works well and when we are getting it right, and we'll try to extend that to other areas and reward your best teachers.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 8\. PGR Student Voice

#### 8.3. King's College London Student Union (KCLSU)

Every student at King's is automatically a member of KCLSU. The KCLSU are a union of students where individuals can meet each other, build communities, share experiences, and make change. The KCLSU's mission is to _make sure every King's student has the best student experience possible._

It is run by students, for its students. They are independent of King's College London and their decisions, policies and what they focus on is decided by its 30,000 student members. You elect Student Officers, Union Development members and Student Representatives you wish to represent you. For more information, visit [here](https://www.kclsu.org/).

* [KCLSU Student Groups](https://www.kclsu.org/groups/) \- This includes activity groups, sports groups, student media, academic associations, student networks and memberships.
title: Postgraduate Research Students' Handbook

text:
### 9\. Welcome to King's App

The Welcome to King's App provides new students with all the information necessary for how to prepare for study at King's, as well as, after arriving at the University. It will have an abundance of information and services that will allow you to embark on your day-to-day activity at King's. _An example of features include:_

* Easy access to a full schedule of Open Day and Welcome Week Events.
* To-do Lists and checklists.
* Overview of the King's services who support you at King's.
* Maps to navigate yourself around the King's campus.
* Platform integration with Twitter and YouTube.
* Student information .

Preview of the App on the App Store:

Download the App on the following platforms:

* [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.guidebook.apps.kcl.android&gl=US)
* [App Store](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/life-at-kings/id1260629407)
* [Desktop](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/mobile)

### 22\. Tuesday 16 July 2024

### Full release notes

* [Moodle 4.2](https://moodledev.io/general/releases/4.2)
* [Moodle 4.3](https://moodledev.io/general/releases/4.3)
* [Moodle 4.4](https://moodledev.io/general/releases/4.4)

### Major features

#### Course page improvements

* Add an activity or resource anywhere on the Course homepage.
* Indent activities or resources one place to the right
* Duplicate sections and benefit from useful permalinks
* Activity names now only display when edit mode is on
* Bulk delete, move, duplicate, hide or change visibility from the footer.
* Smaller activity icons and content arranged to take up less space
* Access activity completion conditions from a dropdown menu
* Access restrictions are hidden until you select Show more
* A group icon displays on hover for activities allowing groups
* A modal window gives teachers quick access to change availability
* Text and media areas can now have names which display in the Course index
* Floating buttons enhance navigation of the Book resource.
* Activies and sections clearly defined.
* With edit mode enabled, teachers click to 'view' a single section.
* Updated activity icons use an accessible colour palette.

#### Gradebook enhancement

* Easily search and filter the Grader report.
* Collapse columns in the Grader report
* View feedback directly in the Grader report
* Sticky footers give quick access to common action buttons and navigation links.
* A modal window allows for more efficient adding and editing of grade items, categories and outcomes in Gradebook setup.

#### Question Bank

* The Question bank now has improved searching and filtering
* Teachers can hide, remove or resize columns in the Question bank.

#### TinyMCE

* TinyMCE is now the default text editor.
* Image handling is more streamlined, giving a better user experience.

#### Quiz and Question bank

* Customise question numbers
* Reopen attempts never submitted.
* Students can see from Quiz navigation when their responses were last saved.
* Teachers can allocate grades to groups of questions in a quiz.

### Updated plugins

* Grid
* Collapsed topics
* Sharing cart

text:
### 1\. Faculty of Natural, Mathematical & Engineering Sciences (NMES)

#### 1.1. Welcome from the Dean

The Faculty of Natural, Mathematical & Engineering Sciences (NMES) includes five departments - Chemistry, Engineering, Informatics, Mathematics and Physics. Our programmes reflect a range of academic disciplines with exciting subjects to study, which will stand you in good stead for the future. We have an excellent record in student employability and our students enjoy a full and personal student experience.

NMES is a centre of excellent research and we are committed to building a safe and sustainable future and changing the world for the better. Rosalind Franklin took her X-ray images of DNA molecules whilst a research fellow at King's in the 1950s. Roger Penrose, who discovered that black hole formation is a robust prediction of the general theory of relativity was a research associate in the Department of Mathematics in the 1960s. Peter Higgs who predicted the existence of the Higgs Boson, studied at King's, as did Michael Levitt, who combined quantum and classical mechanics to understand chemical reactions.
title: Your Faculty & Department

[Context: 1\. Faculty of Natural, Mathematical & Engineering Sciences (NMES) > 1.1. Welcome from the Dean]

... research associate in the Department of Mathematics in the 1960s. Peter Higgs who predicted the existence of the Higgs Boson, studied at King's, as did Michael Levitt, who combined quantum and classical mechanics to understand chemical reactions.

During your time with us, you will be able to experience a range of diverse educational, cultural and social opportunities within your department and across King's. Student societies offer a fantastic way to meet new people and to broaden your knowledge and skills. The [King's Entrepreneurship Institute](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.kcl.ac.uk%2Finnovation%2Finnovation%2Fentrepreneurship-institute%2FEntrepreneurship-Institute-Homepage.aspx&data=01%7C01%7Cmichael.luck%40kcl.ac.uk%7C1329c9976ed34d47b6ce08d71cc200b2%7C8370cf1416f34c16b83c724071654356%7C0&sdata=EsFwnH8TE4NDkr%2Bj809Km7anUjNOCVCF2zEUCC9eha4%3D&reserved=0) provides support and grants for those interested in starting a business and our cultural community brings together artists, cultural partners, staff, students and alumni who all engage with arts and culture in different ways. All students and staff can also choose to enroll on the [Associateship of King's College](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.kcl.ac.uk%2Faboutkings%2Fprincipal%2Fdean%2Fakc%2Fassociateship-of-kings-college&data=01%7C01%7Cmichael.luck%40kcl.ac.uk%7C1329c9976ed34d47b6ce08d71cc200b2%7C8370cf1416f34c16b83c724071654356%7C0&sdata=1HYnan4Z7ai4bgahghUlvDnX6s%2FKEP4q%2BuPMUYmyMN8%3D&reserved=0) course - and I am planning to join this year myself.

Please do look into what King's has to offer and keep an eye on your emails, your webpages and your handbooks. Studying at university provides a chance to think about your aspirations and all you hope to achieve, both now and for the future.

I wish you every success.

Professor Rachel Bearon

Executive Dean of the Faculty of Natural, Mathematical & Engineering Sciences

text:
### 2\. Department of Informatics

#### 2.1. About the Department

The Department of Informatics delivers the highest quality [Undergraduate](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/informatics/study-with-us/undergraduate) and [Postgraduate](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/informatics/study-with-us/taught-degrees) programmes in Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence through the commitment and enthusiasm of its staff within a stimulating, research-led context. You'll interact with world-class experts in many exciting areas of Computer Science.

The Department is 6th in the UK for Computer Science (QS World Rankings).

The Department is organised into [Research](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/informatics/research/index) focusing on algorithms and data analysis, cybersecurity, distributed artificial intelligence, human centred computing, reasoning and planning, and software systems.

Cross-cutting hubs in health, security, urban living and finance provide a virtual clustering of researchers from all parts of the Department, with the fifth hub (autonomous systems) providing a technology focus for the Department as a whole.

Interdisciplinary research and teaching initiatives are being developed with other departments in the [Faculty of Natural, Mathematical & Engineering Sciences](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/nms/index) and across King's.

The Department is based in the historic Bush House, which features state-of-the-art teaching and research spaces. The Strand Campus in the heart of central London, close to the cultural activities of the West End and the South Bank, the major departments of state at Whitehall, and the leading financial institutions of the City, and within easy reach of major transport links. Our central London location is close to top, global companies, such as Google and Amazon, and gives unparalleled access to leading scientific societies, including the Chartered Institute for IT (BCS) and the Institution of Engineering and Technology (IET).

Our staff and students come from all over the world, which provides a rich environment for teaching and research. Diversity is positively encouraged with a number of family-friendly policies in operation, including a core hours policy, the right to apply for flexible working, and support for staff returning from periods of extended absence, for example maternity leave.

The Department boasts a friendly and inclusive culture, with regular social events bringing staff and students together.
title: Your Faculty & Department

### 3\. Student Societies

#### 3.1. Women in Computer Science

The [Women in Computer Science](https://www.kclsu.org/groups/activities/join/20703/) group is a celebration of gender minorities within Computer Science.

We aim to tackle the gender bias in Computer Science and technology by making gender minorities interested in Computer Science at KCL feel supported and encouraged.

Activities include speaker events, workshops, socials and much more!
title: Your Faculty & Department

text:
### 3\. Student Societies

#### 3.2. KCL Tech

KCL Tech is King's College London's dedicated Technology society, bringing together students with different skill sets from various disciplines to create an innovative and supportive community, sharing passions from AI to IoT.

Relaunched in 2022, the society has quickly built up a reputation for having exciting events from hosting Tech Summit - the UK's biggest student-led tech conference & careers fair, to running multiple hackathons (e.g., FirstYearHack, HackLondon) as well as providing weekly workshops and talks with speakers from the industry. What's more, all of our events are free.

We recently launched KCL Tech Academy, our sub-society where we conduct weekly workshops, building students' programming skills from the basics to being capable of writing their own programs. Attendees will receive certificates. You can keep up with us [@kcl_tech_academy](https://instagram.com/kcl_tech_academy) on Instagram!

Check out our [website](http://www.kcltech.co.uk/) for the latest info on our events, informative blogs, and our newly launched Opportunity Board - connecting students with tailored opportunities.

You can also visit KCL Tech's [Facebook](https://fb.com/kcltechsoc), [Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/kcltech/), [X (formally Twitter)](https://x.com/kcltech), and [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/kcltech/) for more information. Be the first to find out about our events on [Huzzle](https://www.huzzle.app/societies/kcl-tech-society).

Become a member on [KCLSU](https://www.kclsu.org/groups/activities/join/22559/).

######

##### Our Signature Events

###### Tech Summit

The UK's biggest student-led tech conference & careers fair that connects students from all over London and nearby areas with over 30 companies, and provides the opportunity attend a range of panels with high profile speakers.

###### HackLondon

HackLondon is KCL Tech's biggest hackathon. 400 students from across London Universities and beyond will compete in teams to complete one of our sponsor set challenges overnight for ~36 hours to compete for amazing prizes.

###### FirstYearHack

###### FirstYearHack

Our mini hackathon aimed at first year students. A perfect opportunity to meet your peers and get your first hackathon experience. Students form teams of 4 and are assigned a 3rd year student as a mentor to help create a solution to the challenge. Participants will also take part in workshops and create a digital pitch.

###### HackTheLion

Aiming to promote cybersecurity, this brand new hackathon will see students join into groups to tackle a series of capture-the-flag challenges.

### 3\. Student Societies

#### 3.3. Engineering Society - KCLES

The [KCLES facebook page](https://www.facebook.com/KCLEngineeringSociety) is formed by a group of King's students who share a common passion for Engineering.

The KCLES is one of the oldest Engineering societies and over the years it has organised countless events and excursions, such as the annual trip to Cumberland Lodge, talks with financial and banking firms, and the occasional ice-skating trip, all with a view of enhancing the our members' experience during their time at King's.

The Society additionally offers assistance to its members in any academic and career concerns relating to Engineering.

[KCL Engineering Society | LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/kcl-engineering-society)

text:
### 3\. Student Societies

#### 3.4. KCL Robotics Society

KCL Robotics Society is known for their educational workshops that teach members engineering skills and provide hands-on experience with innovative projects across different engineering fields; they host and join national competitions and projects, winning awards and inspiring creativity and critical thinking for their members.

All the workshops made by KCL Robotics are accessible to all skill levels, from beginner engineering enthusiasts to experienced engineers, with support from the committee at every step. KCL Robotics provides a safe space for students to explore their interests in robotics and collaborate with like-minded people to create new and inspired creations.

This year, they are focused on maintaining the quality of their current workshops as well as bringing in new ones, alongside collaborations for competitions and workshops with other universities and societies. They are also aiming to increase the size of their members and to provide more socials and opportunities for students to network and socialise.

As always, KCL Robotics hopes to teach and inspire students about all aspects of robotics.

Please visit their [Instagram page](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.instagram.com%2Fkcl.robotics%2F&data=05%7C01%7Chelen.chapman%40kcl.ac.uk%7C9a7b5bbc246344fac53408db95a5f45c%7C8370cf1416f34c16b83c724071654356%7C0%7C0%7C638268312217239393%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=RmDXGyUWEfRYYxPt1gKhj3dVUgkqgN6iBmLZoxWGbSM%3D&reserved=0) for all information and feel free to reach out to them at their email address: [roboticskcl@gmail.com](mailto:roboticskcl@gmail.com)

#### 3.5. King's College London Engineers' Association - KCLEA

[KCLEA](http://www.kclea.org.uk/) is the Alumni Association for all who have studied (or worked in) any form of Engineering at Kings. Its aim is to promote networking and community through a Newsletter LinkedIn and Events. Its members support students with activities with KCLES and WiSTEM, working with the Academics and KCLEA awards Bursaries and Medals.

### 3\. Student Societies

#### 3.6. WiSTEM (Women in STEM)

[KCL Womxn in STEM](https://www.kclsu.org/organisation/kclwistem/) (WiSTEM) is a student-led society providing a supportive environment for students pursuing science, technology, engineering and maths (STEM) degrees at King's College London. The society hosts workshops and networking events to promote professional development. Students benefit from the WiSTEM Mentorship Scheme, wherein a mentee is matched to an experienced mentor from an industry of their choice. Informal get-togethers are also organised to build connection amongst members.

text:
### 3\. Student Societies

#### 3.7. King's Labs (Student Software Engineering Team)

King's Labs (part of King's Business Club) is KCL's one and only pro-bono software engineering team. Founded in 2022, they work with professional clients and innovative companies to develop industry-standard software solutions to level up their business.

They strive to empower students to pursue amazing careers in tech by providing them with practical work experience and networking opportunities through [King's Business Club](https://kingsbusinessclub.co.uk/), the UK's largest business society. For talented, ambitious students who are looking to accelerate their career, King's Labs is the perfect place to develop valuable skills relating to programming and entrepreneurship. They also hold speaker events and competitions throughout the year, as well as King's Labs Academy, their yearly software engineering/coding bootcamp.

They are always looking for new talent to join the team, so feel free to reach out!

You can find them online here:

### 2\. Key contacts and office hours

#### The Departmental Office

The Departmental Office is your first point of contact for any administrative enquiries. We are currently updating our availability details. This information will be ready for the start of teaching. In the meantime please see our email details below.

#### Academics' Office Hours

If you are stuck over a particular problem in a module, the best person to go to for help is the module lecturer. All academic staff in the Department have [advertised office hours during term-time](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/section.php?id=2146629#module-8522785) in which you are able to see them outside lectures. Office hours may take the form of fixed slots each week when lecturers are available in their offices, or may be appointments arranged by email. Many staff will also make appointments with students outside these times if you urgently need to see them.

Teaching staff have a range of teaching, research and administrative commitments in areas additional to teaching, and may not be able to deal with student enquiries immediately, or outside of advertised office hours, so please be patient.

Please note that office hours do not apply during Reading Week. If you need to see a lecturer please make an appointment.

text:
### 3\. Expectations of Behaviour

RESPECT MATTERS - On Campus & Online

We aim to create a space where everyone feels safe, respected, and included, whether you are in a lecture, studying in labs, or being online (giving feedback, sending emails, etc). Everyone is expected to behave respectfully towards one another.

NO PLACE FOR HATE

We don't tolerate any comments or behaviour that is disrespectful or discriminatory - including anything related to someone's gender, sexuality, race, religion, disability, appearance, age, or any other personal characteristics.

Seen something concerning? Experienced something yourself?

Please don't stay silent. If you see or experience any concerning behaviour, talk to someone you trust - like a lecturer, personal tutor, programme administrator, or the Informatics Diversity & Inclusion Co-Chairs: Alfie Abdul-Rahman & David Kohan Marzagão email [informatics-diversity@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:informatics-diversity@kcl.ac.uk)

You can also reach out to a trained Harassment Advisor or your Faculty Welfare Advisor [self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01846/en-us](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01846/en-us)

Or report anonymously via Report + Support [reportandsupport.kcl.ac.uk](https://reportandsupport.kcl.ac.uk/)

More information can be found:

Student Services Online [self-service.kcl.ac.uk/](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/)

[www.kcl.ac.uk/student-life/wellbeing](http://www.kcl.ac.uk/student-life/wellbeing)

[www.kcl.ac.uk/about/bullying-harassment-and-sexual-misconduct](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/about/bullying-harassment-and-sexual-misconduct)

# Your Programme Opportunities

## 1\. Knowledge Exchange Projects

### Knowledge Exchange Projects

All postgraduate taught and third year undergraduate students complete an individual project. This is the module 6CCS3PRJ for UG Computer Science students, 6CCSAPRJ for UG Artificial Intelligence students, and 7CCSMPRJ for PGT students.

In 2025/6, you have the opportunity to complete your individual project as a Knowledge Exchange Project (KEP).

A KEP is a type of individual project with involvement from an external partner. The partner may be from industry, a charity, a public sector organisation or elsewhere in the King's community. They propose the topic, attend some of supervision sessions and provide feedback on your work.

* * *

### Important dates/deadlines

#### PGT (MSc) Students

* PGT Induction Presentation Slides: [Knowledge Exchange Projects MSc Induction 2025-6](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/pluginfile.php/12531710/mod_book/chapter/912736/Knowledge%20Exchange%20Projects%20MSc%20Induction%202025-6.pdf)
* Application window closes 16:00, Thursday 13 November 2025

* * *

### Examples of previous partners and projects

Previous partners include IBM, Terranova, the Open Data Institute, the NHS, Aerogility and FinCrime Dynamics.

Previous projects include:

* Developing natural language processing (NLP) dashboards to make data analysis accessible to non-technical employees.

* Finetuning AI models to create a career support chatbot which made course recommendations to users of a company's reskilling platform.

* Creating an adventure game which integrated with a training platform, to make it more engaging to users.

* Designing an optimisation system using two advanced algorithms, to manage complex task scheduling.

* * *

### Benefits of KEPs

We encourage students to apply for KEP topics that they find interesting! In addition, here are some benefits you can expect:

* Boost employability by designing solutions to current industry needs

* Apply academic knowledge to real-world business challenges

* Develop skills for work like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication

* Expand networks by connecting with professionals and potential employers

* Receive mentorship from industry experts

* Explore careers through exposure to different sectors

* * *

### Hear from previous KEP students

[Context: Your Programme Opportunities > 1\. Knowledge Exchange Projects > Hear from previous KEP students]

* Expand networks by connecting with professionals and potential employers

* * *

### Hear from previous KEP students

Since 2023, 90+ UG and PGT students have completed KEPs. Here's what some of them have to say about the experience.

#### Lavish

'I got exposure to IBM Instana [which has] given me the foundations to work with development operation tools [...] that are usually used by senior software developers.'

'Part of my project actually has been released to the public through Hugging Face. And it's received over 2000 downloads. And this just goes to highlight how in-depth the learning was to achieve this outcome.'

'I've definitely learned DevOps as a whole, which is great for me in my career as a software engineer [...] I've learned how to read massive code bases. [...] I perfected my research skills.'

#### Nathan

'Learning how the things I learnt in my lectures actually came into fruition, as well as working with a company, to learn how to bridge the gap between research and actual output'.

#### Jolene

'You have to really stay on on our toes and see what was really relevant at the moment. Bringing everything together was challenging but I think the entire experience was really, really interesting. And I think this is something that clients would be very attracted to.'

#### Vaibhav

'Projects are proposed because of real-world demand [...] and that has direct benefit when you're doing your research, so there is loads of research going around the industry that will help you write your report.'

'They're very relevant. Whatever you're doing as your dissertation, that's going to be of more value for any job applications that you're going to go out for because most companies are looking for students who have experience in some sort of recent topic. So for my case, it was LLMs and right now, LLMs are one of the biggest topics in our industry.'

* * *

### KEPs impact: presenting projects to wider audiences

#### Presenting at Informatics Industry Showcase

Three UG 2024/5 KEP students had the opportunity to demo their projects at the [Informatics Industry Showcase 2025](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/events/informatics-industry-showcase-2025), sharing their projects in collaboration with IBM, Aerogility, and King's CRESTEM. We welcomed around 200 attendees to the event, from across King's and external organisations.

#### Presenting at King's Festival of AI

#### Presenting at King's Festival of AI

Three MSc students did a demo and presentation of their project at the [2024 King's Festival of AI](https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/personalising-dentistry-education-with-ai-generated-digital-dental-patients-tickets-884881754147?aff=ebdsoporgprofile&_gl=1*3s9m4g*_up*MQ..*_ga*MjcwODM4NTY5LjE3Mzg1NzczMTY.*_ga_TQVES5V6SH*MTczODU3NzMxNi4xLjAuMTczODU3NzMxNi4wLjAuMA..), in partnership with King's FoDOCS. They developed developed prototypes of the usage of Generative Language Models (GLM) in pre-clinical UG Oral Health Care students for transition to clinics.

* * *

### Application Process

If you want to do a KEP, you'll need to apply. Here is a brief outline of the application process and approximate timeline. You'll receive more detailed information via email about applying for KEPs closer to the time.

UG students | PGT students | Event in application process March - May 2025 | N/A | UG (BSc/MSci) students - First-round application: students can optionally apply for KEPs early, for the purpose of receiving feedback on their application. August - September 2025 | October - November 2025 | UG (BSc/MSci) students - Second-round application: students apply for KEPs, taking into account the feedback received in the first round.PGT (MSc) students - Application: students apply for KEPs. September - October 2025 | November - December 2025 | All students willbe requiredto rankall projects(KEPs and non-KEPs) on project ranking software.Please note, you must have applied for KEPs(first, second or both rounds),to be eligible to rank them in yourprojectpreferences. October 2025 | December 2025 | Students are allocated KEPs and the projects begin.

* * *

### Considerations when applying for a KEP

* * *

### Considerations when applying for a KEP

A KEP is a type of individual project, so it is supervised and assessed according to the same timelines, principles, and standards as all individual projects. Your academic supervisor is your main point of contact throughout the project. They will invite the KEP Partner to supervision sessions and share your work with them. The industry stakeholder is expected to attend at least three meetings and provide advice and feedback throughout.

If you are considering completing a KEP, you should be aware of the following considerations:

#### Your project is independent and led by you

You must ensure that the project deliverables meet the requirements of BSc / MSci / MSc projects at King's College London. Your Academic Supervisor and KEP Partner are not responsible for the quality assurance of your work.

#### You are completing your project as part of your degree

Completing a KEP is not the same as completing an internship or any other form of paid work. You are not paid for the work; you carry out the project as part of your degree.

#### The timeline and processes for completing a KEP are the same as for non-KEP projects and are fixed by the Department and College

The academic supervisor and UG Project Coordinator are the main decision-makers throughout the project. You are not permitted to contact domain advisors directly to apply for mitigating circumstances; for this, you should follow the [standard King's processes](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01744/en-us).

#### As per King's policy, you own the Intellectual Property (IP) in your project

IP negotiations between you and external stakeholder may occur after the project completion and this is independent of the project. [King's IP Code of Practice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/assets/policyzone/research/intellectual-property-code-of-practice.pdf).

#### A KEP is a type of individual project, so it is supervised and assessed according to the same principles and standards.

Your academic supervisor is your main point of contact throughout the project. They will invite your domain advisor to supervision sessions and share your work with them. The industry stakeholder will be made aware of the expected level of commitment and is expected to attend at least three meetings and provide advice and feedback throughout.

Whilst highly unlikely, it's possible that the domain advisor becomes unable to fulfil their role during the project. Where necessary, supervisors can rescope KEP projects to minimise dependencies on input, data and/or technologies from the domain advisor.

* * *

### FAQs about the KEP Application Process
title: Your Programme Opportunities
heading_path: Considerations when applying for a KEP

text:
[Context: Your Programme Opportunities > 1\. Knowledge Exchange Projects > FAQs about the KEP Application Process]

... that the domain advisor becomes unable to fulfil their role during the project. Where necessary, supervisors can rescope KEP projects to minimise dependencies on input, data and/or technologies from the domain advisor.

* * *

### FAQs about the KEP Application Process

#### Do I need to apply to complete a KEP?

Yes. We want you to share how your interests, motivations, and experience (personal, academic and/or employment) experiences are a good fit for KEPs.

#### What's required for the application?

You'll need to complete a short application form, plus attach your CV. In the application form, you'll tell us which KEP(s) you are applying for and write a short statement of why you are suitable.

#### Is anything else required?

For UG students only: You must have achieved an average overall grade of 2:1 (60%) or higher across Year 1 and 2 to be eligible for a KEP. We'll check this after you apply, and again after your assessment Period 2 and 3 results have been ratified. Please note, if you can demonstrate exceptional experience, knowledge and/or marks in the specific topic of the KEP(s) you are applying for, along with a rationale as to why you haven't achieved these minimum academic requirements, we will consider your application. If this applies to you, please also include this in your suitability statement(s).

#### How will you evaluate my application?

We will anonymise your application and check you currently meet the academic requirements. A Panel of Informatics academics will evaluate your application based on four criteria:

* Does the applicant demonstrate good understanding of the proposed project topic?

* Do they demonstrate analytical and critical reasoning skills relevant to the topic?

* Do they demonstrate work, voluntary, personal and/or academic experience relevant to the topic?

* Do they appear to have the motivation, preparedness, and potential to successfully complete the project within the set timeframe?

#### I don't think I have enough knowledge/experience/skills to apply - should I?

Yes! We encourage you to apply for KEPs if you think they sound interesting. We're looking for you to meet the criteria above, so please let us know in your application how you think you meet them, in your personal/work/voluntary/academic experience.

#### If I apply, am I guaranteed a KEP?

No. But you must apply if you want to be eligible for a KEP.

* * *

### Essential resources and contact information

text:
##### Your King's IT Account

When you enrol, you'll need to set up a [King's IT account](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/it/new-to-kings "Go to King's IT for new students").

#####

##### Student Number

As a student you'll be given a student number which can be used for:

* Your personal identity number - it helps staff to find your details

* Found on your student ID card/offer letter

* Helpful to add to your email signature

* The username you need to log on to King's email, Student Records, KEATS, Student Services Online and other IT platforms

##### King's Email**
**

Once you've set up your IT account, you'll be able to access your King's email account via Microsoft Outlook. Your email address will usually follow the format firstname.lastname@kcl.ac.uk

Please check your email every day - this is the main method of communication between you and the University/department (all important information such as any timetable changes, assessment and course updates will be sent via email). Make sure you use this account for all correspondence with us.

##### KEATS

KEATS is the King's [Virtual Learning Environment](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virtual_learning_environment). It's where you'll find your course learning materials, activities and assessments (and it is where you are reading this now!). You will have a KEATS area for each module you study.

Once you enrol, you'll have access to KEATS areas for your modules as well as resources for: [Careers](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=41567), [Academic Study Skills (KASL)](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=62576) and more.

KEATS will be used to in a different way by each academic, but will often include the following:

* Module syllabus and assessment details

* Name of the module leader

* Name/s of any other academics teaching the module

* Handouts and lecture slides

* Recordings of lectures

* Interactive features such as forums and quizzes

* A bibliography or online reading list

* An assignment submission area where you submit your work and access your feedback

* Discussion forum, including a course announcements area

You can to KEATS as soon as you've got you King's username (e.g. K1234567) and password.

* Discussion forum, including a course announcements area

You can to KEATS as soon as you've got you King's username (e.g. K1234567) and password.

You'll find more information in the [Getting Started on KEATS area](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=2461951 "Getting started with KEATS").

##### Student Records**
**

[Student Records](https://mykcl.kcl.ac.uk/) is an online platform that allows you to manage your personal information at King's. You'll automatically get access but you'll need to enrol and set up your IT account first.

It's important to keep your personal information up to date.

Student Records is where you'll be able to see:

* Your student number (e.g. 2012345)

* Your candidate number for the current academic year (e.g B123456)

* Your module registrations

* The name of your personal tutor

* Your results, once they've been ratified

* How to change your address

Go to the [Administration section of Student Services Online](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/category/?id=CAT-01059 "Student records information on Student Services Online") for further details about Student Records.

Teaching Flipped Classroom
Most of our modules use the 'flipped classroom' approach.

In the flipped classroom approach, we deliver
module content through videos and other materials via KEATS.

This makes space in the in-person sessions for
you to benefit maximally from the world-class experts in the Department - through discussions, exercises, and
Q&A sessions.

Flipped classroom teaching has been proven in the education-research literature to significantly
improve student learning and outcomes.

We are looking forward to offering great opportunities for intensive
interaction with your teachers and peers.

Timetable
Your timetable is found via KCL MyTimetable.

Students are expected to complete their resit or deferred assessments in the August reassessment period.

All
exams take place in person, and students who need to complete an exam should ensure they are able to be

We know how much our students value the on-campus experience here at King's and we encourage you to
make the most of teaching, learning, networking, and socialising on our campuses.

Teaching Flipped Classroom
Most of our modules use the 'flipped classroom' approach.

In the flipped classroom approach, we deliver
module content through videos and other materials via KEATS.

This makes space in the in-person sessions for
you to benefit maximally from the world-class experts in the Department - through discussions, exercises, and
Q&A sessions.

Flipped classroom teaching has been proven in the education-research literature to significantly
improve student learning and outcomes.

We are looking forward to offering great opportunities for intensive
interaction with your teachers and peers.

Language modules
You can take a language module as a "replacement" for one of your Informatics modules.

The following
Taking modules outside the conditions apply:
Department and/or outside
your programme - You cannot take additional modules which will take your credit load above 120 credits.

- The language module must replace one of your Informatics Level 6 optional modules.

- You cannot also take a Level 7 module if you take a language module.

- You cannot replace a Core or Compulsory module.

Only optional modules can be replaced.

- Linguistic modules cannot be taken.

- The language module can be at any language level and will be included in your degree calculation as a
Level 6 module.

Language modules should have been requested during the Module Selection period in March/April 2025 and
cannot be requested now.

Alternatively, you can take a language module as "not for credit" (this does not count towards your degree) as
an extra-curricular subject.

Please visit the King's Language Centre's website for more information.

If you are taking a Level 7 module(s) in 2025/26, please note:
- The pass mark for these modules is 50 (not 40).

- If you do not obtain a mark of 50%, this may impact your ability to gain your degree.

Marks within the
40-49 range could be considered for condonement, but students are only permitted 30 credits of
Taking Level-7 modules condoned fails across their degree and only 15 of these credits can be condoned in their final year.

- You cannot replace a Core or Compulsory module.

Only optional modules can be replaced.

- We cannot guarantee that there will not be timetable clashes for students choosing Level 7 modules.

Priority is given to preventing Level 6 module clashes for Year 3 students.

- Consideration should be given to further study.

If you are considering applying for a masters degree,
some modules are compulsory on the masters programmes.

This will mean students cannot apply to
the MSc if a compulsory module has already been taken.

Requests to take Level 7 modules should have been made during the Module Selection period in March/April
2025 and cannot be requested now.

The list of available modules are provided on the Undergraduate Programmes page for your programme of
study.

Students are expected to complete their resit or deferred assessments in the August reassessment period.

All
exams take place in person, and students who need to complete an exam should ensure they are able to be
present in London during the August period to sit their assessments.

We know how much our students value the on-campus experience here at King's and we encourage you to
make the most of teaching, learning, networking, and socialising on our campuses.

Teaching Flipped Classroom
Most of our modules use the 'flipped classroom' approach.

In the flipped classroom approach, we deliver
module content through videos and other materials via KEATS.

This makes space in the in-person sessions for
you to benefit maximally from the world-class experts in the Department - through discussions, exercises,
and Q&A sessions.

Flipped classroom teaching has been proven in the education-research literature to
significantly improve student learning and outcomes.

We are looking forward to offering great opportunities
for intensive interaction with your teachers and peers.

Your timetable is found via KCL MyTimetable.

This
could be one of your lecturers, your personal tutor, a programme administrator, the diversity & inclusion co-
chairs (Alfie Abdul-Rahman & Kathleen Steinhöfel) at informatics-diversity@kcl.ac.uk, a trained harassment
advisor, or any member of staff you feel comfortable talking about it to.

Report + Support has now launched for all members of the King's community to report incidences of

text:
### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.2. BSc Computer Science: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### **Educational Aims:**

The main aims of the BSc Computer Science programme offered by the Department include the following:

* To equip you with systematic knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of computing, so that you may be able to pursue a professional career and/or postgraduate study successfully.
* To offer you opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare you to play a creative role in the community.
* To develop your understanding and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and motivate you to pursue continual professional development.
* To ensure that you acquire an understanding of your professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Basic theoretical concepts of Computer Science.
* Hardware and systems platforms (operating systems, computer architecture, networks and communications etc.).
* Programming concepts and various programming paradigms.
* Systematic development of large scale software (systems analysis, design, implementation and evaluation).
* Modern information technology (selected topics from: parallel/distributed computing, network computing, internet technology, agents, automated verification and reasoning, artificial intelligence, data and knowledge bases, computer graphics, multimedia, information security etc.).
* Business and management techniques that are relevant to software engineering.
* The professional and ethical responsibilities of software engineers.
* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills including planning and conducting research, analysing and solving computing problems, designing systems, components or processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include a full range of software engineering skills (specification, design and implementation of computer-based systems, evaluation of systems and design trade-offs), effective contribution to development teamwork, preparation of technical presentations, reports, and documentations, oral presentations, effective use of the scientific literature.
title: Undergraduate Programmes

text:
[Context: 1\. Undergraduate Programmes > 1.2. BSc Computer Science: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes > **Learning Outcomes:**]

... software engineering skills (specification, design and implementation of computer-based systems, evaluation of systems and design trade-offs), effective contribution to development teamwork, preparation of technical presentations, reports, and documentations, oral presentations, effective use of the scientific literature.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including effective communication (in writing, verbally and through diagrams and graphs), applied mathematical skills (logic, geometry, modelling, discrete mathematics), working as a member of a team, learning independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry, learning effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development in a wider context throughout their career.

text:
### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.6. BSc Computer Science with a Year in Industry: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### **
**

##### **Educational Aims:**

The main aims of the BSc Computer Science with a Year in Industry programme offered by the Department include the following:

* To equip you with systematic knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of computing, so that you may be able to pursue a professional career and/or postgraduate study successfully.
* To offer you opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare you to play a creative role in the community.
* To develop your understanding and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and motivate you to pursue continual professional development.
* To ensure that you acquire an understanding of your professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.
* To develop your awareness of the relation between your studies and the needs of the software engineering industry through a year-long regulated placement in an appropriately selected organisation.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Basic theoretical concepts of Computer Science.
* Hardware and systems platforms (operating systems, computer architecture, networks and communications etc.).
* Programming concepts and various programming paradigms.
* Systematic development of large scale software (systems analysis, design, implementation and evaluation).
* Modern information technology (selected topics from: parallel/distributed computing, network computing, internet technology, agents, automated verification and reasoning, artificial intelligence, data and knowledge bases, computer graphics, multimedia, information security etc.).
* Business and management techniques that are relevant to software engineering.
* The professional and ethical responsibilities of software engineers.
* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills including planning and conducting research, analysing and solving computing problems, designing systems, components or processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.
title: Undergraduate Programmes

text:
[Context: 1\. Undergraduate Programmes > 1.6. BSc Computer Science with a Year in Industry: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes > **Learning Outcomes:**]

... processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include a full range of software engineering skills (specification, design and implementation of computer-based systems, evaluation of systems and design trade-offs), effective contribution to development teamwork, preparation of technical presentations, reports, and documentations, oral presentations, effective use of the scientific literature.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including effective communication (in writing, verbally and through diagrams and graphs), applied mathematical skills (logic, geometry, modelling, discrete mathematics), working as a member of a team, learning independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry, learning effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development in a wider context throughout their career.

### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.8. BSc Computer Science with Management: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the BSc Computer Science with Management programme include the following:

* To equip you with systematic knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of the core aspects of computing, so that you may be able to pursue a professional career and/or postgraduate study successfully.

* To equip you with understanding of core concepts related to organisations, economics and marketing, required for playing leading roles in organisations.
* To offer you opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare you to play a creative role in society.
* To develop your understanding and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and in organisations and motivate you to pursue continual professional development.
* To enable you to combine analytical and modelling skills with programming and software engineering skills that you acquire, so that you might contribute to the development and deployment of information technology in the organisations where you find yourself.
* To ensure that you acquire an understanding of your professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Basic theoretical concepts of Computer Science.
* Hardware and systems platforms (computer architecture, networks and communications, etc.), programming concepts and various programming paradigms.
* Systematic development of large-scale software (systems analysis, design, implementation and evaluation).
* Modern information technology (parallel/distributed computing, network computing, internet technology, automated verification and reasoning, databases, computer graphics, multimedia, information security etc.).
* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.
* Core areas of management science, such as: economics, organisational behaviour, marketing, human resource management, business strategy and accounting.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills including planning and conducting research, analysing and solving computing problems, designing systems, components or processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.

##### **Educational Aims:**

The main aims of the BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year Abroad programme offered by the Department include the following:

* To equip students with state-of-the-art knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of computer science, so that they might be able to pursue a professional career and/or postgraduate study successfully.

* To offer students opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare them to play a creative role in society.

* To ensure that students acquire an understanding of their professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.
* To ensure that students are aware of the impact of computing technology on business and organizational strategy.
* To offer students an opportunity to spend one year of study at an approved overseas academic institution, and supplement their studies with topics that are possibly included in foreign curricula as well as to experience a foreign culture socially and professionally.

* Core areas of management science, such as: economics, organizational behaviour, marketing, human resource management, business strategy and accounting.
* The professional and ethical responsibilities of software engineers.
* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.

text:
### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.12. BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year in Industry: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### **Educational Aims:**

The main aims of the BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year in Industry programme offered by the Department include the following:

* Equip students with state-of-the-art knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of computer science, so that they might be able to pursue a professional career and/or postgraduate study successfully.
* Equip students with core concepts related to organizations, economics and marketing to enable them to play a leading role in their professional career in organizations.
* Offer students opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare them to play a creative role in society.
* Develop students' understanding and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and in organizations and motivate them to pursue continual professional development.
* Enable students to combine analytical and modelling skills with programming and software engineering skills that they acquire, so that they might contribute to the development and deployment of information technology in the organizations where they can find themselves.
* Ensure that students acquire an understanding of their professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.
* Ensure that students are aware of the impact of computing technology on business and organizational strategy.
* Develop Students' awareness of the relationship between their studies and the needs of software engineering industry through a year-long regulated placement in an appropriately selected organisation

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

* Basic theoretical concepts of Computer Science

* Hardware and systems platforms (computer architecture, networks and communications, etc.), programming concepts and various programming paradigms

* Systematic development of large scale software (systems analysis, design, implementation and evaluation)

* Modern information technology (parallel/distributed computing, network computing, internet technology, automated verification and reasoning, natural language processing, databases, computer graphics, multimedia, information security etc.)

* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context

* Core areas of management science, such as: economics, organizational behaviour, marketing, human resource management, business strategy and accounting

* The professional and ethical responsibilities of software engineers.
title: Undergraduate Programmes

text:
[Context: 1\. Undergraduate Programmes > 1.12. BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year in Industry: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes > **Learning Outcomes:**]

... the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context

* Core areas of management science, such as: economics, organizational behaviour, marketing, human resource management, business strategy and accounting

* The professional and ethical responsibilities of software engineers.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills including planning and conducting research, analysing and solving computing problems, designing systems, components or processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include a full range of software engineering skills (specification, design and implementation of computer-based systems, evaluation of systems and design trade-offs), effective contribution to development teamwork, preparation of technical presentations, reports, and documentations, oral presentations, effective use of the scientific literature.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including effective communication (in writing, verbally and through diagrams and graphs), applied mathematical skills (logic, geometry, modelling, discrete mathematics), working as a member of a team, learning independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry, learning effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development in a wider context throughout their career.

text:
### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.14. MSci Computer Science: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

The aims of the MSci Computer Science programme are:

* To equip you with systematic knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of computer science, so that you might be able to pursue a professional career and/or further research successfully.
* To equip you so that you may demonstrate originality in the application of knowledge and a thorough understanding of how established research techniques can be used to create and interpret knowledge in computer science.
* To enable you to evaluate critically current research and the methodologies that are used to pose and explore new hypotheses.
* To offer you opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare you to play a creative and leading role in the professional and research community.
* To develop your critical awareness and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and motivate you to pursue continual professional development and further research.
* To ensure that you acquire an understanding of your professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides opportunities for you to develop and demonstrate knowledge and understanding and skills in the following areas:

* Theoretical concepts of Computer Science.
* Hardware and systems platforms (operating systems, computer architecture, networks and communications etc.).
* Programming concepts and various programming paradigms.
* Systematic development of large scale software (systems analysis, design, implementation and evaluation).
* In-depth knowledge on modern information technology subjects (parallel/distributed computing, network computing, internet technology, agents, natural language processing, automated verification and reasoning, artificial intelligence, data and knowledge bases, computer graphics, multimedia, information security etc.).
* In-depth knowledge on advanced specialist and interdisciplinary subjects, such as advanced programming techniques, parallel and distributed algorithms, safety-critical systems, algorithms for computational molecular biology etc.
* Business and management techniques that are relevant to software engineering.
* The professional and ethical responsibilities of software engineers.
* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual, practical and transferable skills such as:
title: Undergraduate Programmes

text:
[Context: 1\. Undergraduate Programmes > 1.14. MSci Computer Science: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes > **Learning Outcomes:**]

... ethical responsibilities of software engineers.
* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual, practical and transferable skills such as:

* Plan, conduct and report a programme of original research.
* Analyse critically and solve computing problems.
* Design a system, component or process to meet a need.
* Be creative in the solution of problems and in the development of designs.
* Evaluate designs, processes and products critically, and make improvements.
* Integrate and evaluate information and data from a variety of sources.
* Take a holistic approach in solving problems and designing systems, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations.
* Be self directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

**Practical skills:**

* Specification and design.
* Implementation of computer-based systems.
* Evaluation of systems and design trade-offs.
* Identification and definition of research ideas.
* Effective contribution to development teamwork.
* Effective group organisation and leadership.
* Preparation of technical presentations.
* Production of technical reports and documentation.
* Giving oral presentations.
* Effective use of the scientific literature.
* Effective note-taking.
* Effective use of computational tools and packages.

**Generic/transferable skills:**

* Communicate effectively (in writing, verbally and through diagrams and graphs) with specialist and non-specialist audiences.
* Apply mathematical skills (logic, geometry, modelling, discrete mathematics).
* Work as a member of a team and lead a team.
* Transfer techniques and solutions from one problem domain to another.
* Use information technology.
* Retrieve information using catalogues and search engines.
* Manage resources and time.
* Learn independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry.
* Learn effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development and further research in a wider context throughout their career.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations systematically and creatively.
* Exercise initiative and personal responsibility.

text:
### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.16. BSc Artificial Intelligence (with a Year Abroad): Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

#####

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the BSc Artificial Intelligence programme offered by the Department include the following:

* To equip students with systematic knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of AI
* To offer students opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills.
* To develop students' understanding of the changing role of AI in society and their professional and ethical responsibilities.

For the Study abroad pathway, in addition to the above:

* To offer students the opportunity to spend one year of study at an approved institution, typically in the USA, and supplement their studies with topics that are possibly included in foreign curricula, as well as experience foreign culture socially and professionally.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Basic theoretical concepts of computer science, especially those underpinning artificial intelligence.
* Machine learning and neural networks.
* Optimisation, search, and AI Planning.
* Formal logic and reasoning underpinning.
* Data science: data management and analysis.
* Agent-based systems and coordination.
* Engineering approaches to AI -based systems.
* Programming concepts and reasoning, various programming paradigms.
* Foundations of robotics.
* Ethical and social implications of AI.
* Professional and ethical responsibilities of developers of AI-based systems.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills including planning and conducting research, analysing and solving computing problems, designing systems, components or processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include a full range of AI skills (specification, design and implementation of AI-based systems, evaluation of systems and design trade-offs), effective contribution to development teamwork, preparation of technical presentations, reports, and documentations, oral presentations, effective use of the scientific literature.

### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.18. BSc Artificial Intelligence with a Year in Industry: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the BSc Artificial Intelligence with Year in Industry programme offered by the Department include the following:

* To equip students with systematic knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of AI
* To offer students opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills.
* To develop students' understanding of the changing role of AI in society and their professional and ethical responsibilities.
* To develop your awareness of the relation between your studies and the needs of the AI industry through a year-long regulated placement in an appropriately selected organisation.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Basic theoretical concepts of computer science, especially those underpinning artificial intelligence.
* Machine learning and neural networks.
* Optimisation, search, and AI Planning.
* Formal logic and reasoning underpinning.
* Data science: data management and analysis.
* Agent-based systems and coordination.
* Engineering approaches to AI -based systems.
* Programming concepts and reasoning, various programming paradigms.
* Foundations of robotics.
* Ethical and social implications of AI.
* Professional and ethical responsibilities of developers of AI-based systems.
* Actual theoretical and practical problems that arise in real world AI-based systems.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills including planning and conducting research, analysing and solving computing problems, designing systems, components or processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include a full range of AI skills (specification, design and implementation of AI-based systems, evaluation of systems and design trade-offs), effective contribution to development teamwork, preparation of technical presentations, reports, and documentations, oral presentations, effective use of the scientific literature.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including effective communication (in writing, verbally and through diagrams and graphs), applied mathematical skills (logic, geometry, modelling, discrete mathematics), working as a member of a team, learning independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry, learning effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development in a wider context throughout their career.

### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.20. MSci Artificial Intelligence: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the MSci programme offered by the Department include the following:

* To equip students with systematic knowledge and experience of the theory and practice of AI
* To offer students opportunities to develop analytical and practical transferable skills.
* To develop students' understanding of the changing role of AI in society and their professional and ethical responsibilities.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Basic theoretical concepts of computer science, especially those underpinning artificial intelligence.
* Machine learning and neural networks.
* Optimisation, search, and AI Planning.
* Formal logic and reasoning underpinning.
* Data science: data management and analysis.
* Agent-based systems and coordination.
* Engineering approaches to AI -based systems.
* Programming concepts and reasoning, various programming paradigms.
* Foundations of robotics.
* Ethical and social implications of AI.
* Professional and ethical responsibilities of developers of AI-based systems.
* In-depth knowledge on advanced subjects in AI, such as advanced machine learning, AI planning, data science, AI security, AI ethics and philosophy, software engineering for AI-based systems, etc.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills including planning and conducting research, analysing and solving computing problems, designing systems, components or processes to meet given requirements, evaluating designs, processes and products, and making improvements, integrating and evaluating information and data from a variety of sources, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact. You will be able to take a holistic approach in solving problems and designing systems, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact. You will also be able to make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations and be self directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include a full range of AI skills (specification, design and implementation of AI-based systems, evaluation of systems and design trade-offs), effective contribution to development teamwork, preparation of technical presentations, reports, and documentations, oral presentations, effective use of the scientific literature.

### 1\. Undergraduate Programmes

#### 1.21. Choosing your degree title in your final year

From academic year 2017-8, final year students on the majority of our Computer Science programmes are invited to choose their degree title. Students on BSc Computer Science, BSc Computer Science with a Year Abroad, BSc Computer Science with a Year in Industry, BSc Computer Science with Management, BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year in Industry, BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year Abroad and MSci Computer Science are eligible.

Visit the [KEATS page](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=50926 "KEATS ") for more information and to submit your choice. This includes details about the specialisations, the criteria, how to select one, how this ties in with your module selections, a letter to support students on a Tier 4 visa, and some Frequently Asked Questions.

All final year students have been given access to this KEATS page. If you cannot gain access, please email your Programme Officer via [informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk).

Please read the documentation carefully and then submit your choice via the MS Form link on the dedicated KEATS page.

### 1\. Postgraduate Programmes

#### 1.2. MSc Advanced Computing Educational Aims and Learning Objectives

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the MSc Advanced Computing programme offered by the Department include the following:

* To equip students with systematic knowledge and experience of computing at an advanced level, so that they may be able to pursue professional careers and/or further research successfully.
* To equip students so that they may demonstrate originality in the application of knowledge and a thorough understanding of how established research techniques can be used to create and interpret knowledge in computing.
* To enable students to evaluate critically current research and the methodologies that are used to pose and explore new hypotheses.
* To offer BSc graduates opportunities to develop further analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare them to play a creative and leading role in the professional and research community.
* To develop students' critical awareness and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and motivate them to pursue continual professional development and further research.
* To ensure that students acquire an understanding of their professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* A range of advanced computer science subjects, selected by the student as those most relevant to his/her interests and career aspirations.
* Algorithms, theories, methods and programming techniques relevant to the chosen subjects.
* Computational problems arising in various application domains.
* In-depth knowledge of a range of modern information technology subjects.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:

* Plan, conduct and report a programme of original research.
* Analyse critically and solve computing problems.
* Design a system, component or process to meet a need.
* Be creative in the solution of problems and in the development of designs.
* Evaluate designs, processes and products critically, and make improvements.
* Integrate and evaluate information and data from a variety of sources.
* Take a holistic approach in solving problems and designing systems, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations.
* Be self-directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include:

* Be self-directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include:

* Specification, design and implementation of computer-based systems.
* Evaluation of systems and design trade-offs.
* Identification and definition of research ideas.
* Preparation of technical presentations.
* Production of technical reports and documentation.
* Giving oral presentations.
* Effective use of the scientific literature.
* Effective note-taking.
* Effective use of computational tools and packages.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including:

* Communicate effectively (in writing, verbally and through diagrams and graphs) with specialist and non-specialist audiences.
* Apply mathematical skills (logic, geometry, modelling, discrete mathematics).
* Transfer techniques and solutions from one problem domain to another.
* Use information technology.
* Retrieve information using catalogues and search engines.
* Manage resources and time.
* Learn independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry.
* Learn effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development and further research in a wider context throughout their career.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations systematically and creatively.
* Exercise initiative and personal responsibility.
