### 1\. Postgraduate Programmes

#### 1.4. MSc Advanced Software Engineering: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the MSc Advanced Computing programme offered by the Department include the following:

* Equip students with systematic knowledge and experience of computing at an advanced level, so that they may be able to pursue professional careers and/or further research successfully.
* Equip students so that they may demonstrate originality in the application of knowledge and a thorough understanding of how established research techniques can be used to create and interpret knowledge in computing.
* Enable students to evaluate critically current research and the methodologies that are used to pose and explore new hypotheses.
* Offer BSc graduates opportunities to develop further analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare them to play a creative and leading role in the professional and research community.
* Develop students' critical awareness and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and motivate them to pursue continual professional development and further research.
* Ensure that students acquire an understanding of their professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies in a wide and varied range of contexts.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Design and analysis of software systems.
* Advanced software engineering techniques.
* Computational problems arising in the context of software engineering and methods of solving them.
* Systematic development of large-scale software (systems analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation).
* In-depth knowledge of a range of modern technology subjects.
* The professional and ethical responsibilities of software engineers.
* The role of the software engineer in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:

* Plan, conduct and report a programme of original research.
* Analyse critically and solve computing problems.
* Design a system, component or process to meet a need.
* Be creative in the solution of problems and in the development of designs.
* Evaluate designs, processes and products critically, and make improvements.
* Integrate and evaluate information and data from a variety of sources.
* Take a holistic approach in solving problems and designing systems, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations.
* Be self-directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include:

text:
### 1\. Postgraduate Programmes

#### 1.6. MSc Artificial Intelligence Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

This MSc is aimed at graduates in science and engineering who have prior experience of computer programming and mathematics in their first degree and who wish to obtain specialised training in Artificial Intelligence. Specific aims are:

* To provide students with theoretical knowledge of a range of techniques for developing intelligent software and machines.
* To provide students with practical knowledge and expertise sufficient to evaluate, design and build artificially intelligent systems.
* To enhance students programming, analytical, critical, problem solving and transferable skills.
* To prepare students for careers in industry or for further research work.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* The essential facts, concepts, principles and theories relating to artificial intelligence.
* The science and mathematics underpinning the above.
* Gain awareness of current research in artificial intelligence.
* Appropriate theory, and techniques, for the design, implementation and evaluation of artificially intelligent systems.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:

* Ability to evaluate, and compare, methodologies used in artificial intelligence.
* Ability to understand and analyse problems and identify suitable approaches to solving them.
* Ability to undertake critical evaluation (both theoretical and empirical) of alternative solutions.
* Ability to plan, conduct and report on a programme of research and development.
* Ability to understand, appraise and evaluate relevant literature.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include:

* Ability to apply theoretical knowledge to solve practical problems.
* Ability to specify, design and construct artificially intelligent systems, using appropriate tools, and to document all stages of this process.
* Ability to select appropriate methodologies and adapt and improve them as necessary.
* Ability to critically evaluate the performance of an intelligent system.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including:

* Management of own learning and development, including time management, and organizational skills.
* Ability to work independently on a substantial project.
* Ability to use appropriate methods for literature searching and information retrieval.
* Ability to critically analyse and evaluate appropriate literature.
* Technical writing and presentation skills.
title: Postgraduate Programmes

text:
### 1\. Postgraduate Programmes

#### 1.8. MSc Computational Finance: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the MSc Computational Finance programme offered by the Department include the following:

* In-depth understanding of the models and methodologies used in quantitative and computational finance, and their underlying assumptions and limitations.
* The practical skills needed to analyse financial data, and to apply the appropriate analysis and modelling techniques for financial decision making.
* An appreciation for the professional, ethical and legal responsibilities of financial practitioners, and the societal and ethical implications of financial modelling and decision-making on society at large.
* An understanding of the central role that computational models, and information and communication-technologies more generally, play in modern financial markets.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* The fundamental principles of quantitative and computational financial modelling, and how these are used to make investment decisions.
* Knowledge of the major theoretical tools and theories of finance, and their relevance and application to theoretical and practical problems.
* An understanding of the relationship between financial theory and empirical testing, and application of this knowledge to the appraisal of the empirical evidence in at least one major theoretical area.
* An ability to interpret data generated in financial markets.
* The technologies and engineering methodologies employed in the provision of electronic financial services.
* An in-depth understanding of computer programming, scientific computing, techniques for analysing financial data and modelling financial markets.
* The application of financial analysis, modelling or financial software engineering in a specific domain of application.
* An understanding of financing arrangements and governance mechanisms and structures of business entities, and an appreciation of how theory and evidence can be combined to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of such arrangements.
* An appreciation of the nature of the contexts in which finance can be seen as operating, including knowledge of the institutional framework necessary for understanding the role, operation and function of markets and financial institutions.
* An understanding of the factors influencing the investment behaviour and opportunities of private individuals.
* An understanding of financial service activity in the economy, and the factors that are changing these activities over time, and evidence can be employed to aid such understanding.
* An ability to evaluate the technical, societal and management dimensions of computer systems.
* A knowledge and understanding of advanced aspects of computer systems and their use.
* An understanding of professional, legal, social, cultural and ethical issues related to computing and an awareness of societal and environmental impact.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:
title: Postgraduate Programmes

text:
[Context: 1\. Postgraduate Programmes > 1.8. MSc Computational Finance: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes > **Learning Outcomes:**]

... advanced aspects of computer systems and their use.
* An understanding of professional, legal, social, cultural and ethical issues related to computing and an awareness of societal and environmental impact.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:

* Plan, conduct, and report a programme of original research.
* Analyse critically, and apply, quantitative and computational models to financial data.
* Integrate and evaluate information and data from a variety of sources.
* Take a holistic approach in tackling investment problems, applying professional judgement to balance desired decision objectives such as risk, return, and regulatory constraints.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations.
* Be self-directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include:

* Critical evaluation of the methodology, methods and models used in financial modelling and decision making.
* Organise and manage projects involving the use of financial data, financial modelling or financial software engineering.
* Identification and definition of research ideas.
* Preparation of technical presentations.
* Giving oral presentations.
* Effective use of the scientific literature.
* Effective note-taking.
* Effective use of computational tools and statistical software and packages.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including:

* Critical evaluation of arguments and evidence.
* Analysis, filtering and evaluation of data, and drawing reasoned conclusions concerning structured and, to a more limited extent, unstructured problems from a given set of data and from data acquired by the student
* Location, extraction and analysis of data from multiple sources, including acknowledging and referencing of sources
* Numeracy, including the processing and analysis of financial and other numerical data and the appreciation of statistical concepts at an appropriate level
* Using contemporary information and communications technology for the acquisition, analysis and communication of financial information.
* Communication, including presenting quantitative and qualitative information, together with analysis, argument and commentary, in a form appropriate to the intended audience, and oral as well as written presentation.
* Working with others.

#### 1.9. MSc Cyber Security: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### **Educational Aims:**

The main aims of the MSc Cyber Security programme offered by the Department include the following:

* Equip students with systematic knowledge and experience of the theoretical foundations and practice of cyber security solutions at an advanced level, so that they may be able to pursue professional careers and/or further research successfully.
* Equip students so that they may demonstrate originality in the application of knowledge and a thorough understanding of how established research techniques can be used to create and interpret knowledge in cyber security.
* Enable students to evaluate critically current research and the methodologies that are used to pose and explore new hypotheses.
* Offer BSc graduates opportunities to develop further analytical and practical transferable skills and prepare them to play a creative and leading role in the professional and research community.
* Develop students' critical awareness and appreciation of the changing role of information technology in society and motivate them to pursue continual professional development and further research.
* Ensure that students acquire an understanding of their professional and ethical responsibilities and of the impact of computing technologies and security solutions in a wide and varied range of contexts.

##### **Learning Outcomes:**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* Algorithm design techniques for cryptography and secure solutions.
* Mathematical tools for analysing the performance of algorithms for security solutions.
* Designing secure systems and applications.
* Advanced programming techniques for secure applications.
* Socio-technical cyber security.
* Systematic cyber security testing and risk assessment and management.
* Systematic development of secure systems and applications.
* In-depth knowledge of the following subjects: distributed computing security, network security, cyber crime, forensics and language based security.
* Management techniques, standards and certifications that are relevant to cyber security.
* The professional and ethical responsibilities of cyber security professionals and researchers.
* The role of the security expert in the development and application of computing technology and solutions in a global context.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:

* Evaluate designs, processes and products critically, and make improvements.
* Integrate and evaluate information and data from a variety of sources.
* Take a holistic approach in solving problems and designing systems, applying professional judgements to balance risks, costs, benefits, safety, reliability, aesthetics and environmental impact.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations.
* Be self-directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include:

* Specification, design and implementation of secure systems.
* Testing of systems and design trade-offs.
* Identification and definition of research ideas.
* Effective contribution to development teamwork.
* Effective group organisation and leadership.
* Preparation of technical presentations.
* Production of technical reports and documentation.
* Giving oral presentations.
* Effective use of the scientific literature.
* Effective note-taking.
* Effective use of computational tools and packages.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including:

* Communicate effectively (in writing, verbally and through diagrams and graphs) with specialist and non-specialist audiences.
* Apply mathematical skills.
* Transfer techniques and solutions from one problem domain to another.
* Use information and security technology.
* Retrieve information using catalogues and search engines.
* Manage resources and time.
* Learn independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry.
* Learn effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development and further research in a wider context throughout their career.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations systematically and creatively.
* Exercise initiative and personal responsibility.

text:
### 1\. Postgraduate Programmes

#### 1.12. MSc Data Science: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes

##### Educational Aims:

The main aims of the MSc Data Science programme offered by the Department include the following:

* In-depth understanding of the general principles of the computational and statistical methodologies and methods used in data science, and their underlying assumptions and limitations.
* The practical skills needed to effectively assemble, collate, store, manage, and retrieve the data required for data science projects and organise the data in a form that is suitable for analysis
* The critical judgement to decide, given typical project objectives and constraints, the appropriate statistical and computational data exploration or analysis techniques to employ data management, analysis and decision-making, and to evaluate critically data science activities and projects.
* The ability to select, to employ, and to develop appropriate software tools to apply computational and statistical data analysis techniques.
* The skill set required to plan, undertake, manage, and critically assess a data science project.
* An appreciation for the professional, ethical and legal responsibilities of the data scientist and the societal and ethical implications of data science projects on stakeholders and on society at large.
* An appreciation of standard conceptual or scientific models in at least one domain of application of data science techniques.

##### **Learning Outcomes**

The programme provides a knowledge and understanding of the following:

* The general principles of the computational and statistical techniques that are fundamental to data science, and how these affect the data science practice.
* Methodologies employed in large-scale data science projects.
* The modelling methods used to formulate data science problems.
* In-depth understanding of statistical analysis techniques, computer programming, simulation, database systems, data warehousing, information retrieval, data preparation, data visualisation, data mining and machine learning.
* The assumptions and limitations underlying methods and techniques and how these affect their application.
* Advanced computational, statistical or mathematical techniques for data science.
* Data science practice and applications in a specific domain of application.
* The professional, ethical and legal responsibilities of the data scientist, as well as the technical, societal, legal, ethical and management dimensions of data science projects.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:
title: Postgraduate Programmes

text:
[Context: 1\. Postgraduate Programmes > 1.12. MSc Data Science: Educational Aims and Learning Outcomes > **Learning Outcomes**]

... of application.
* The professional, ethical and legal responsibilities of the data scientist, as well as the technical, societal, legal, ethical and management dimensions of data science projects.

When studying on this programme you will also develop a number of intellectual skills:

* Plan, conduct and report a programme of original research.
* Analyse critically and solve data science problems.
* Choose computational and statistical data science modelling and analysis methods with critical judgement.
* Be creative in the modelling, analysis and solution of data science problems.
* Integrate and evaluate information and data from a variety of sources.
* Take a holistic approach in tackling data science problems, applying professional judgements to balance desired decision objectives, risks, costs, benefits, efficiency, effectiveness, safety, reliability, aesthetics, and environmental impact.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations.
* Be self-directed and original in solving problems, and act autonomously in undertaking research.

Practical skills obtained when graduating from this programme include:

* Critical evaluation of the methodology and methods used in data science projects.
* Effective data management, including collection, cleaning, collation, organisation, storage, and retrieval of data.
* Organise and manage data science projects.
* Identification and definition of research ideas.
* Preparation of technical presentations.
* Production of technical reports and documentation.
* Giving oral presentations.
* Effective use of the scientific literature.
* Effective note-taking.
* Effective use of computational tools and statistical software and packages.

You will also acquire a number of transferable skills including:

* Communicate effectively (in writing, verbally, and through diagrams and graphs) with specialist and non-specialist audiences.
* Apply computational and mathematical skills.
* Transfer techniques and solutions from one problem domain to another.
* Use information technology.
* Retrieve information using catalogues and search engines.
* Manage resources and time.
* Learn independently in familiar and unfamiliar situations with open-mindedness and in the spirit of critical enquiry.
* Learn effectively for the purpose of continuing professional development and further research in a wider context throughout their career.
* Make decisions in complex and unpredictable situations systematically and creatively.
* Exercise initiative and personal responsibility.

text:
### 2\. Programme Transfer

##### Programme transfer requests from outside department students

We are unable to accept requests from outside department students to transfer onto our programmes in 2025/26.

##### Programme transfer requests - Informatics students

If you wish to transfer onto a different programme from the one you are registered on, please make a request by emailing your Programme Officer: [informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:Informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk) for undergraduate students, or [informatics-pgt@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:Informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk) for postgraduate students.

You should also consult your personal tutor to discuss your with to transfer and the lead of the programme you are leaving and of the programme you are joining. The department(s) will be able to confirm whether the change of programme is appropriate for the stage of study you are at, and that you have the appropriate entrance qualifications for the proposed programme.

##### Transfers between undergraduate programmes

All transfer requests will need to be reviewed by a number of approvers and are not guaranteed. Students who have a visa to study will need to speak with the [Visa & International Student Advice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/student-life/visa) team.

* No transfers are permitted between BSc Computer Science and BSc Artificial Intelligence.
* Transfer requests from BSc Computer Science to BSc Computer Science with Management must be requested by the end of the first week of teaching. This is on a 'one in, one out' basis so is not guaranteed. All students will go on a waiting list on a first come, first serve basis.
* Transfer requests to and from the 'with Management' programmes cannot be undertaken after the end of Year 1.
* **Transfers between BSc Computer Science and MSci Computer Science:**

###### _Transferring from BSc to MSci_

Transfers from BSc Computer Science to MSci Computer Science are permitted, and you should contact your Programme Officer ([informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:Informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk)) who can assist you with this process.

The latest you can request a transfer to the MSci will normally be 1st of May of your third year. If you wish to transfer after this date, you will be added to a waiting list so that we can check that you meet the progression requirements.

Before requesting a transfer, you should consider the following:
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
[Context: 2\. Programme Transfer > Transfers between undergraduate programmes > _Transferring from BSc to MSci_]

... 1st of May of your third year. If you wish to transfer after this date, you will be added to a waiting list so that we can check that you meet the progression requirements.

Before requesting a transfer, you should consider the following:

1. You should meet with your personal tutor or a relevant academic to discuss whether the MSci programme meets your future goals and ambitions.
2. Do you require a student visa to study a further year (Year 4)?___For further advice, please contact the[Visa & International Student Advice team](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/student-life/visa)._
3. How are you funded? (E.g., Student Finance England, a sponsor, or self-funded)? _If you are funded through Student Finance England (SFE), we advise you to contact the Funding Advice team ([funding@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:funding@kcl.ac.uk)) to find out whether you can be funded for a further year. _
4. Will you have your accommodation sorted out for a further year of study?

Please be aware that transfers are subject to students meeting the relevant [progression requirements](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665193&chapterid=866037).

###### _Transferring from MSci to BSc_

Transfers from MSci Computer Science to BSc Computer Science are permitted. If you are studying on a student visa, you should contact the [Visa & International Student Advice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/student-life/visa) team for further advice.

The latest point at which you can transfer from the MSci to BSc to graduate in July of your third year, will be the 1st of May (of your third year). Requests to transfer to the BSc after this date will still be processed, however, you will be invited to the January Graduation Ceremony rather than the July Ceremony.

Transfer requests for other Computer Science programmes can be made. Please contact your Programme Officer at [informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:Informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk).
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
### 2\. Programme Transfer

#### 2.1. Interruption

If you decide to formally interrupt your studies for a period of time, you should consult your programme lead or personal tutor. Tuition fees will be charged pro-rata until the online form is completed via [Student Records](https://mykcl.kcl.ac.uk).

You should discuss your proposed interruption with [Student Advice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/campuslife/services/student-advice-support/how/Changes-in-your-personal-circumstances "Student Advice") and read the information on their web pages before submitting your application - if you are a Tier 4 visa holder this is mandatory as an interruption of studies can have serious implications for your visa status.

You can also take a look at the information and support available on [Student Services Online](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01214/en-us "https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01214/en-us").

Once you have made the decision, you can complete the appropriate online Student Change of Circumstances form, which can be accessed from [Student Records](https://mykcl.kcl.ac.uk).
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
### 2\. Programme Transfer

#### 2.2. Withdrawal

If you decide to withdraw completely from the programme, you should consult the programme lead or personal tutor. You should also read the information provided on the [Student Advice](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/campuslife/services/student-advice-support/how/Changes-in-your-personal-circumstances "Go to Student Advice") pages and [Student Services Online](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01213/en-us "Go to Student Services Online "). Tuition fees will be charged pro-rata until the date of confirmation of withdrawal is received. If you receive financial support, either as a personal stipend or for fees, you must inform your financial sponsors of your intention to withdraw.

You should consider interrupting your studies before withdrawing completely, as this may be a more appropriate course of action.

Once you have made the decision, you can complete the appropriate online Student Change of Circumstances form, which can be accessed from [Student Records](https://mykcl.kcl.ac.uk).
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
### 3\. Tuition Fees & Debtors

Tuition fees are payable for each year of your study. For information on how much your fees will be and how you can pay, the Student Fees Hub has a list of [frequently asked questions](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/aboutkings/orgstructure/ps/finance/fees/faq) for students with fee enquiries. These FAQs will guide you to the best place to get your questions answered.

There is also fees, funding and money advice on [Student Services Online](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/category/?id=CAT-01000 "Student Services Online").

If you are a PhD student, you are required to pay fees each year until your MPhil/PhD degree has been submitted. If you are required to resubmit your thesis, you will pay an additional administrative fee for your period of registration.

The College and Faculty are unable to release any information to students who have outstanding debts to the College. This includes student status letters and examination results.
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
### 4\. Progression, Compensation and Condonement requirements

The progression and award requirements define how many credits and modules you need to pass to progress from one year of study to the next and to be awarded your degree.

In order to progress from one year to the next, or to be awarded your Bachelors with Honours or an Integrated Masters with Honours, you should pass 120 credits in each year of study. If you do not pass 120 credits in a year of study, but you have satisfied some progression criteria, then you may be allowed to progress to the next year of study, carrying over some fails.

The minimum progression requirements for students enrolled on a full-time undergraduate programme are as follows:

Students enrolling inSeptember 2025and later:Click here Students enrolling inSeptember 2024:Click here Students who enrolled inSeptember 2022andSeptember 2023:Click here Students who enrolled inSeptember 2020andSeptember 2021:Click here Students who enrolledprior to September 2020: Please contact the Department office(informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk) for details on progression requirements.

If you are unable to meet the requirements as set out above, having exhausted all reassessment attempts, you may be eligible for a lower qualification e.g. Certificate on Higher Education, Diploma of Higher Education or an Ordinary Degree. Any such award will not be classified.

Further information can be found in the [Academic Regulations](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academicregulations/kings-academic-manual).
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
### 4\. Progression, Compensation and Condonement requirements

#### 4.1. Condonement

You may find that some of your modules allow for condonement (also known as condoned fail) or compensation. This means you may be awarded a qualification where you are carrying a specific number of failed credits, but your overall performance is of a good standard. You will not be reassessed if you meet the condonement or compensation criteria. Condonement and compensation can only be applied to modules that are not core (i.e. core modules must be taken and passed) and the module marks fall within a certain range. Further information can be found [Academic Quality & Regulations](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academicregulations) and navigate to the **King's Academic Manual** section.

For further information, please visit the relevant page for your level of study below:

[Condonement and Compensation for Undergraduate students (BSc & MSci)](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665193&chapterid=866039)

[Condonement for Postgraduate students (MSc)](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665193&chapterid=866040)
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
### 4\. Progression, Compensation and Condonement requirements

#### 4.2. Condonement and Compensation for Undergraduate students (BSc & MSci)

##### What is a condoned fail and/or compensation?

A condoned fail or compensation means you are given credit for a module under certain circumstances even though you have not passed it. This credit will be counted towards the award of a degree, but **cannot be counted towards progression**.

Compensation is applicable for students who have enrolled from September 2022 to September 2024.

##### What are the circumstances to be awarded a condoned fail or compensation?

**For students who have enrolled in September 2021 or earlier****:**

A condoned fail can be awarded for modules delivered by the Department of Informatics where the overall module mark is in the range of:

* 33-39 for Level 4 modules
* 1-39 for Level 5 and Level 6 modules
* 1-49 for Level 7 modules

Modules from other departments may not follow these criteria. Instead, they may allow a range of 33-39 for Level 4, 5 and 6 and 40-49 for Level 7. If you are taking a module from outside of Informatics, please contact the host department for further information.

**For students who have enrolled from September 2022 to September 2024****:**

Compensation is awarded for non-core modules delivered by the Department of Informatics at Level 4 where a module mark is in the range of 30-39, **and** when a student has exhausted all their reassessment opportunities.

A condoned fail can be awarded for modules delivered by the Department of Informatics where the overall module mark is in the range 30-39 for Level 5 and Level 6, and 40-49 for Level 7.

**For students who have enrolled from September 2025 onwards:**

Due to accreditation requirements, only 30 credits of non-core modules may be condoned across the duration of your degree.

In order to meet the expectations of the Professional Body that accredits the programme, the University definition of 'compensation' is not applied here. Instead condonement will be applied for modules at level 4 as well as 5, 6 and 7.

##### **When will my modules be condoned?**

**For students who have enrolled in September 2021 or earlier****:**

text:
[Context: 4\. Progression, Compensation and Condonement requirements > 4.2. Condonement and Compensation for Undergraduate students (BSc & MSci) > **When will my modules be condoned?**]

... condonement will be applied for modules at level 4 as well as 5, 6 and 7.

##### **When will my modules be condoned?**

**For students who have enrolled in September 2021 or earlier****:**

'Condonement' for a module is only permitted, at the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board. Modules are only condoned when a student has exhausted all their reassessment opportunities, or when condoning modules would allow students to graduate with the same classification they would achieve had they passed those modules.

**For students who have enrolled from September 2022 to September 2024****:**

At Level 4, a module will be compensated by the Assessment Sub-Board if the mark is within the required range and the student:

* Achieves a pass mark in all core modules (see your [programme page](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665187) to check which modules are core);
* Achieves a pass mark in a minimum of 90 credits overall;
* When a student has exhausted all their reassessment opportunities.

At Level 5, 6 and 7, a module will be condoned by the Assessment Sub-Board if the mark is within the required range. Please refer to the '**What are the circumstances to be awarded a condoned fail or compensation?** ' section above.

**For students who have enrolled in September 2025 and onwards****:**

Due to accreditation requirements, only 30 credits of non-core modules may be condoned across the duration of your degree.

In order for a non-core module to be condoned you must have achieved a minimum mark of 30 for Informatics modules at levels 4, 5 and 6 or 40 for modules at level 7. Modules delivered by other departments outside of the faculty may be condoned in accordance with standard University regulations

##### **What if a module has a component with a qualifying mark?**

Some modules will have qualifying marks, which means you are required to obtain a minimum mark for particular components (e.g. coursework or exam) in order to pass the module. If you fail to obtain the qualifying mark, in one or more components, of a module, then you will have failed the module, even if your overall module mark is equal to or higher than the pass mark (40 for Levels 4, 5 and 6 modules, and 50 for Level 7 modules).

##### **How many credits can be condoned?**

**For students who have enrolled from September 2019 to September 2021****:**

##### **How many credits can be condoned?**

**For students who have enrolled from September 2019 to September 2021****:**

Up to 45 credits of condoned fails are permitted on 3- and 4-year undergraduate degree programmes. No more than 15 of these credits can condoned in your final year of study.

**For students enrolling in, or after, September 202****2:**

Up to 30 credits of condoned fails are permitted on 3- and 4-year undergraduate degree programmes. No more than 15 of these credits can be condoned in your final year of study.

##### **Are there any modules that cannot be condoned?**

Core modules cannot be condoned and must be passed. These include 5CCS2SEG***** , 6CCS3PRJ and 7CCSMGPR, cannot be condoned.

***** From September 2022 and onwards,**** 5CCS2SEG is a core module.

**Students taking BSc Computer Science with Management, BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year Abroad, or BSc Computer Science with Management and a Year in Industry:**

Condonement requirements will vary for KBS Joint Honours modules and these may not follow the same regulations as Informatics modules. For further information, please contact the KBS Joint Honours team via: [KBS-JointHonours@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:KBS-JointHonours@kcl.ac.uk).

##### **Students on MSci Computer Science who started before 2013**

Students who started their MSci degree before September 2013 must pass at least 120 credits at Level 7. These 120 credits cannot include any condoned fails.

Students who started their MSci degree from September 2013 and onwards can have 30 credits of condoned fails allowed at Level 7, (they must have a minimum of 90 credits at Level 7 excluding condoned fails).

##### **What if I fail a module and it is not condoned?**

If you fail a module at the final attempt and this is not condoned (usually because you did not achieve a mark in the condonable range in any attempt, or you already have too many condoned credits), then you will no longer be able to continue with your studies at King's. There is no option to take any further resits of any modules, or to retake the year.

### 4\. Progression, Compensation and Condonement requirements

#### 4.3. Condonement for Postgraduate students (MSc)

##### **What is a condoned fail?**

A condoned fail means you are given credit for a module under certain circumstances even though you have not passed it. This credit will be counted towards the award of a degree.

Condoned fails (credit awarded for marks in the range 1-49) are permitted, at the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board.

##### **How many credits can be condoned?**

Up to 30 credits (two modules) of condoned fails are permitted on Masters programmes. However, the condonement rules change if you have selected Level 6 modules.

Students who take a Level 6 module (third year undergraduate module), will have this included in the maximum number of condoned fails permitted.

##### **Taking Level 6 modules and how they impact on condonement**

Further information on how Level 6 modules will affect your degree can be found in the [Academic Regulations](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academicregulations/kings-academic-manual "https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academicregulations/kings-academic-manual").

The rules and impact of taking Level 6 modules are as follows:

* You will be permitted to take up to two Level 6 modules (30 credits) as optional modules, so long as your degree programme offers them.
* Level 6 modules have a pass mark of 40.
* Level 6 modules are at a lower educational attainment level. Therefore, in order to show that you are undertaking a Masters level of education, you must compensate for this is another way. This compensation is that you will have a limited number of condonable credits available to you at Level 7:
title: Progression and Programme Transfers

text:
[Context: 4\. Progression, Compensation and Condonement requirements > 4.3. Condonement for Postgraduate students (MSc) > **Taking Level 6 modules and how they impact on condonement**]

... level. Therefore, in order to show that you are undertaking a Masters level of education, you must compensate for this is another way. This compensation is that you will have a limited number of condonable credits available to you at Level 7:

-**No Level 6 modules taken:** Two Level 7 modules can be condoned.
-**One Level 6 module taken:** One Level 7 module can be condoned, and the Level 6 module must be passed. If the Level 6 module is not passed, you cannot have a Level 7 module condoned and all Level 7 modules must be passed with a mark of at least 50.
\- **Two Level 6 modules taken:** No Level 7 modules can be condoned. All Level 7 modules must be passed with a mark of at least 50, and all Level 6 modules must be passed with a mark of at least 40.

* All students must have achieved a minimum mark of 50 in at least 150 credits at Level 7
* Condonement is at the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board and is not guaranteed. Students should aim to pass every module.
* Taking Level 6 modules does not reduce your overall degree average
* Please be aware that if you have not passed an assessment at the first attempt and a resit is required, only the pass mark can be obtained for the second attempt at the assessment (40 for Level 6 modules and 50 for Level 7 modules). For example, if you achieve a mark of 60 for a resit at the second attempt, you will only be granted a mark of either 40 (for Level 6 modules) or 50 (for Level 7 modules). This is to reflect that you have taken the assessment twice. Students should consider that this would reduce the overall average of the degree.

For further details on the requirements of the MSc degree, please see the [Award requirements (for PGT programmes) page](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665194&chapterid=866043 "https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=8665194&chapterid=866043").

##### **Are there any modules that cannot be condoned?**

Core modules (7CCSMPRJ) cannot be condoned.

##### **When will my modules be condoned?**

##### **Are there any modules that cannot be condoned?**

Core modules (7CCSMPRJ) cannot be condoned.

##### **When will my modules be condoned?**

Modules with a mark that could be condonable may be offered as a resit, because there may be a chance that you can improve your degree classification (the weighted average of all your marks). If you do not take the resit for the module your original mark will stand, and the Assessment Sub-Board will decide whether to condone this at its November meeting. This happens in November because modules can only be condoned when you have exhausted all your attempts, or in order for you to graduate (i.e. you wouldn't be forced to wait and take a second attempt at a module, if you can be awarded your degree straight away with a condoned fail).

The Assessment Sub-Board will be able to confirm the condonement of modules only after the submission of the Individual Project. This means that when ratified results are published in the summer, condonable marks will appear as fails, as their condonement can only be ratified by the final Assessment Board meeting in November.

### 1\. Award Requirements for UG programmes

#### 1.1. Safety Net Policy for Covid-19 Pandemic

A 'safety net' policy is a means to ensure that no student will be disadvantaged academically by circumstances associated with the COVID-19 pandemic. Below are details of how this will be implemented for students in NMS. [Please also see the main Safety Net FAQs](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/coronavirus/students/assessment#safetynet "Go to main Safety Net FAQs").

##### First year undergraduate students

Your grades from assessments taken in 2020/21 will not contribute towards your final degree classification. Only marks obtained from year two onwards will be used in determining your degree. This is not a part of the safety net policy, but is due to changes in the academic regulations for undergraduate awards.

##### Second year undergraduate students

Your grades from your first-year assessments, taken in 2019/20 (or based on teaching taken in 2019/20) will not contribute towards your final degree classification. Only marks obtained from year two onwards will be used in determining your degree. Additional checks will be put in place at the point of considering your final degree outcome to ensure you are not disadvantaged by this decision.

##### Safety Net for other undergraduate students

The safety net average will be the year average of the year of study prior to 2019/20. For most students this will be the year average for 2018/19.

For students who were abroad or in industry placements for that year, the results from the last year spent studying at King's will be used.

Your Safety Net average and self-service letter will be available via Student Records at the same time as your results providing you have completed all modules.

**Note the safety net policy applies to assessments taken in the 2019/0 academic year only.** For information on 2020/1 assessments, please see the [Fair Assessment Policy page](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=3259811&chapterid=431415).

##### Safety Net for Postgraduate taught students

Students who were registered to a postgraduate programme of study in 2019/20 and who have an overall score within two percent of a higher classification boundary (i.e. 68/58) will be automatically upgraded to the higher classification (Pass with Distinction/Pass with Merit) where at least 60 credits are in the higher classification or above, in a minimum of two modules.
title: Award and Classification

text:
### 1\. Award Requirements for UG programmes

#### 1.2. Safety Net Average: Frequently Asked Questions

##### COVID-19 related assessment policies

The College has implemented a range of policies designed to mitigate against the impact that COVID pandemic had on students who were registered to programmes of study in the 2019-20 and/or 2020-21 academic years.

##### Safety Net Policy for students who were registered to a programme of study in 2019-20: Frequently Asked Questions

###### Using these FAQs

These FAQs have been designed to answer questions that you might have as current students. They should be read in conjunction with the [King's Coronavirus FAQs](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.kcl.ac.uk%2Fcoronavirus&data=01%7C01%7Cjoanna.gillis%40kcl.ac.uk%7Cfbacc8ba637d40cf6cbd08d827e44ebf%7C8370cf1416f34c16b83c724071654356%7C0&sdata=wehmKKT1ZICUSXuodbZm9uTbOFw%2FKbgJJ5erSEeAsGU%3D&reserved=0) and alongside your handbook and other relevant information.

We will continue to update these FAQs as more information becomes available, so please check back regularly. If you have a question that is not answered here, and you think that it would be valuable to add to these FAQs then please contact the NMS Quality Assurance Manager [joanna.gillis@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:joanna.gillis@kcl.ac.uk)

###### What do you mean by Safety Net Policy?

During the 2019-20 academic year we had to rapidly respond to the challenges that the COVID-19 pandemic placed upon our teaching and assessment activities. The Safety Net Policy was a package of measures designed to ensure that you were not unfairly disadvantaged by those circumstances.

###### First-year progression:

If you were a first-year undergraduate students in 2019-20, it was agreed that the grades you achieved in 2019-20 would not be used to calculate your final degree classification. You would simply be assessed for progression this year on a Pass/Fail basis. Additional checks will be made when you complete your award, to make sure that you have not been disadvantaged by this decision.

###### Safety Net Average:
title: Award and Classification

text:
[Context: 1\. Award Requirements for UG programmes > 1.2. Safety Net Average: Frequently Asked Questions > Safety Net Policy for students who were registered to a programme of study in 2019-20: Frequently Asked Questions > Safety Net Average:]

... your final degree classification. You would simply be assessed for progression this year on a Pass/Fail basis. Additional checks will be made when you complete your award, to make sure that you have not been disadvantaged by this decision.

###### Safety Net Average:

It was agreed that a Safety Net Average would be calculated using the weighted average of the grades you achieved for your studies in 2018-19, or the last year that you had been in College in the case that you had interrupted your studies or been abroad/on placement in 2018-19. For example, if your weighted average for 2018-19 was 59%, your Safety Net Average for 2019-20 would also be 59%. If your 2019-20 year average was lower than the Safety Net Average, it was agreed that your final degree classification would be calculated using your Safety Net Average.

###### Postgraduate students:

Careful consideration was given to the development of a safety net for postgraduate taught programmes. The structure of these programmes was taken into account, as was the fact that the majority of programmes run for one year only, meaning that, for most students, there were no results from a previous year that could be used to calculate a safety net average. To ensure that postgraduate students were not disadvantaged by the pandemic, it was agreed that a 2% rule would be implemented, whereby if your final grade was within 2% of the boundary for an award with merit (i.e., 58/59%) or an award with distinction (68/69%) and you had at least 60 credits in the higher classification from two or modules, the College would have automatically conferred the higher award.

###### Where can I see my Safety Net Average?

You will be able to view your Safety Net Average in the 'View Module Results' page in Student Records. Where a Safety Net Average has been calculated, it will appear alongside your weighted average for the year.

###### What do you mean by weighted average for the year?

Each year of your studies contributes a different weighting to your [final classification.](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/campuslife/acservices/academic-regulations/assets-19-20/t30.pdf) Level 4 is weighted at 1; Level 5 at 3, the best 90 credits at Level 6 is weighted at 5, and the remainder of level 6 is weighted at 3. To determine your weighted year average, the sum of the credit for each module studied is multiplied by the value it contributes and the marks achieved. This is then divided by the sum of the credit and its value to create your weighted year average.

[Context: 1\. Award Requirements for UG programmes > 1.2. Safety Net Average: Frequently Asked Questions > Safety Net Policy for students who were registered to a programme of study in 2019-20: Frequently Asked Questions > What do you mean by weighted average for the year?]

...3. To determine your weighted year average, the sum of the credit for each module studied is multiplied by the value it contributes and the marks achieved. This is then divided by the sum of the credit and its value to create your weighted year average.

###### I was on a Year Abroad/Year in Industry in 2018-19, which grades were used to calculate my Safety Net Average?

We used the grades from modules taken in the last year that you were studying at the College, for example your year 2 studies in 2017-18.

###### How will my Safety Net Average be used in my award classification?

Provided your Safety Net Average is higher than your weighted year average for 2019-20, it will be used to calculate your final [cumulative score](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/campuslife/acservices/academic-regulations/assets-19-20/t30.pdf), which is used to determine your degree classification. For example:

If you are a current finalist student me who has passed all modules at the first attempt, attained a weighted year average in 2018-19 of 73%, and in 2019-20 - your second year\- you only attained a weighted year average of 65%, the calculations would be as follows:

* **201****8, Year 1:** 120 credits at level 4 with an overall grade of 73% weighted at 1
* **201****9, Year 2:** 120 credits at level 5 with a Safety Net Average applied of 73% weighted at 3
* **20****20, Year 3:** 90 credits at level 6 with an overall grade of 70% weighted at 5 plus 30 credits at level 6 with an overall grade of 63 weighted at 3.

Your overall cumulative score would be 71 %, giving you a First-Class award.

You have therefore benefitted from the application of the Safety Net Average; had your final classification been calculated using your 2019-20 year average of 65%, your overall cumulative score would have been brought down to 68%. Dependent upon the number of credits you achieved at level 6 in the first-class category, you may have been considered for an upgrade to First-class, but you may well have only achieved a 2.1.

###### Were individual module grades amended to reflect my Safety Net Average?

No. The grades that will appear on your transcript and Higher Education Achievement Record (HEAR) will be the actual grades awarded. A separate statement will be produced with your HEAR/Transcript to explain the safety net.

###### What was not included in the Safety Net Average calculation?

... on your transcript and Higher Education Achievement Record (HEAR) will be the actual grades awarded. A separate statement will be produced with your HEAR/Transcript to explain the safety net.

###### What was not included in the Safety Net Average calculation?

Modules which you **deferred** from 2018-19 and took during 2019-20 without tuition were not included in the Safety Net Average. If you considered that your deferred assessments had been impacted by COVID-19 during 2019-20, you were able to take advantage of the Mitigating Circumstances policy to defer them again until you felt better able to submit them.

###### I re-took modules on a resit basis (without tuition) during 2019-20, were they included in my Safety Net?

Yes. Once you had completed all modules which you are taking as a second attempt, including any deferrals where you had previously failed the modules, these were included in the calculation of your Safety Net Average

###### If I decided to defer for a year or interrupt my studies, when will my Safety Net Average be calculated?

The Safety Net Average will only be calculated once you have completed all outstanding modules from 2019-20.

###### I am a current finalist; how do I know if my Safety Net Average has been used to calculate my final award?

If your Safety Net Average is higher than your weighted average for 2019-20, it will be used to calculate your [cumulative score](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/campuslife/acservices/academic-regulations/assets-19-20/t30.pdf) (C-Score). If it is lower, your final award will be calculated according to the standard rules.

###### I was a first-year undergraduate student in 2019-20, why don't I have a Safety Net Average?

As you were in your first year, the College did not have any data with which to make a comparison. To ensure that you were not disadvantaged by the Coronavirus pandemic, it was agreed that the grades you achieved in 2019-20 would not be used to calculate your final degree classification. You would have been assessed for progression to year 2 on a Pass/Fail basis.

###### How do you know that the grades I achieve in my first year as an undergraduate would not have resulted in my achieving a higher classification?

... final degree classification. You would have been assessed for progression to year 2 on a Pass/Fail basis.

###### How do you know that the grades I achieve in my first year as an undergraduate would not have resulted in my achieving a higher classification?

For students who were in their first year in 2019-20, once you are in your final year, the College will carry out a further check. If your final cumulative score falls one percent below the boundary for borderline consideration, that is you achieve a score of either 47%, 57% or 67%, the Assessment Board will look back at your first-year grades for 2019-20 and determine whether including them would make a difference.

They will consider whether: a) including your grades will push your score into the borderline category (that is 48/49%, 58/59% and 68/69%) or b) including your grades will automatically move you into the higher classification. If a) applies your results will be considered by the Assessment Board, who will determine whether you merit a discretionary uplift to the next classification. If b) applies, you will automatically be awarded the higher classification.

###### I was not a first-year undergraduate student in 2019-20, why do I not have a Safety Net Average?

The Safety Net Average can only be calculated once you have completed all your modules for 2019-20. It may be that you have deferrals or referrals outstanding, which you will complete during the current academic year.

###### What should I do if I have questions about or do not agree with the Safety Net Average calculation?

You should, in the first instance, contact your department so that we can try and address your questions or concerns informally. If you are not satisfied following this, you can submit an appeal using the usual [appeals process](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/aboutkings/orgstructure/ps/acservices/conduct/academic-appeals).

text:
### 2\. Award Requirements for PGT programmes

##### Postgraduate Taught degrees (MSc)

To be awarded an MSc you will need to gain 180 credits, with an overall weighted average of 50% over all taught modules.

All modules must either be:

* Modules at Level 7 with a pass mark of 50% or more;

**_OR_**

* Condoned fails, which includes all modules at Level 6.

A mark of 50 is needed in at least 150 credits, including the dissertation.

The overall weighted average mark is calculated by multiplying each module's mark by each module's amount of credits (15 for taught modules and 60 for the Individual Project), thus obtaining an overall total. The overall total must then be divided by the total credit volume, which is of 180 credits. The Assessment Sub-Board has the right to make relevant adjustments.

All Level 6 modules must be passed with a mark of 40 or more.

If reassessment is required then you will be permitted only one reassessment attempt. It should be noted that only the pass mark can be obtained for the second attempt at assessment (40% for Level 6 modules, 50% for Level 7 modules). For example, if you resit an exam and obtain a mark of 60, you will only be granted a mark of 40 (for Level 6 modules) or 50 (for Level 7 modules). This is to reflect that you have taken the assessment twice. Students should consider that this would reduce the overall average of the degree.

In order to be eligible for the award of a Masters degree with **Merit** , a candidate should achieve a weighted average mark across all modules of 60 to 69 inclusive.

In order to be eligible for the award of a Masters degree with **Distinction** , a candidate should achieve a weighted average mark of 70 or above.

All marks for all credit gained during the programme will count towards an award calculation, this includes condoned credit.

###### Exit awards:

Where a student has failed to satisfy the examiners in all elements of a programme and:

a) has exhausted any available reassessment opportunities or has terminated their studies early; and

b) has met the criteria for a lower level or lower volume exit award as detailed in the programme specification for the award on which they are registered

The Assessment Sub-Board may, in accordance with College criteria, recommend the award of the relevant exit qualification.

text:
### 2\. Module Changes

You may be able to make a change to your optional module selections (you cannot change core or compulsory modules) at set times by completing a **Module Change Request Form**.

When the initial module selection deadline closes, we use the data gathered to request appropriate teaching spaces. Therefore, the initial module selection period __ is the only chance to guarantee you can take the modules you select _._ Therefore, please think carefully about your choices during module selection, as there is a strong possibility you won't be able to change them later.

###### **Before requesting a module change, please read the following carefully:**

If your module change is accepted, it is your responsibility to catch up on any work missed on the new module and you will not receive mitigation for any work you have missed up to the date that your module change has been approved _(this is not considered a mitigating circumstance)_. Nor will you be given extensions on future deadlines on the grounds of changing modules.

If you do not agree with the above, _you should remain on the module you originally chose and notify us so that we can cancel your request_.

To ensure that you do not miss any assessment deadlines, please do the following:

* As soon as you have submitted a request to change module, self-enrol on the KEATS module page and read all the information about assessments and ensure you complete any coursework that is required.
* We would advise that you do this for the module you are enrolled on as well as the module you would like to change too in case your request cannot be accepted or you change your mind.
* Check the timetable for the module on [My Timetable](https://mytimetable.kcl.ac.uk/ "My Timetable"). If you require help checking the timetable, please use this [guide](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/pluginfile.php/12113071/mod_book/chapter/866055/Find%20timetable%20-%20user%20guide%20for%20students.pdf).

Requests received outside of these dates will **not** be accepted. The Department receives many module amendment requests so this process may take some time. This is not an automated process so the change will not be seen instantly on your Student Records account or timetable. [Context: 2\. Module Changes > **Process:**]

... is not an automated process so the change will not be seen instantly on your Student Records account or timetable. It requires Programme Officers to manually update your records so it could take a few days after the deadline for the change to show.

##### **Process:**

1. Students will complete an Undergraduate or Postgraduate Taught Module Change Request form. Please note that the form will be only be available during the periods above.
2. After the deadline, requests will be reviewed by the UG and PGT Programme Teams.
3. If the change is possible, the UG and PGT Programme Teams will action your module change(s). You will not receive confirmation when this is completed, so keep checking your Student Records account.
4. The module change will be shown on your Student Records account and reflected on your timetable (this can take a few days).

We cannot guarantee approval of your module change, as they are dependent on the timetable, class size, and space available on the module.

Before making a module change request, it is your responsibility to check that the change would not result in a timetable clash; that you have a balance of modules across the semesters (where possible), and that you meet your specific programme requirements. Assuming the new module is within your programme specification, you should start engaging with teaching immediately. If you require allocation to a small group tutorial this will usually be completed within a week.

You are strongly recommended to discuss module changes with your personal tutor.

Please ensure you are using the appropriate Module Change Request Form:

* [Undergraduate Module Amendment Form ](https://forms.office.com/e/1NHQiESrum)
* [Postgraduate Taught Module Amendment Form ](https://forms.office.com/e/ZULfyiyKkn)

text:
### 1\. Teaching

#### 1.1. Study skills and support

##### King's Academy

We want you to reach your full potential while you are at King's, whether you are learning to write your first university laboratory report, starting a team project or aiming to ace your presentations. King's Academy offer a range of self-access online resources, workshops and one-to-ones to help you develop your academic skills, including scientific writing and statistical skills.

They offer one-to-ones led by Academic Skills Tutors from a range of subjects, including from your faculty. Take a look at [their profiles](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=72741 "their profiles") to see what skills they can help you with, including scientific writing, statistics and general academic skills.

**EXPLORE:** [King's Academic Skills for Learning](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=62576 "King's Academic Skills for Learning") resources and find out more about our workshops and one-to-ones.

##### Libraries & Collections

**WATCH:** Your introduction to the resources, services and support available to you from Libraries and Collections: [Libraries & Collections Overview - King's College London (kcl.ac.uk)](https://media.kcl.ac.uk/media/Undergraduate+and+Postgraduate+Taught+Students+-+Libraries+%26+Collections+Overview+2022-23/1_vvb391ta "Libraries & Collections Overview - King's College London \(kcl.ac.uk\)")

##### King's Language Centre

The [King's Language Centre](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/language-centre "King's Language Centre") supports the studies of King's postgraduate students by offering credit-bearing modules (Language Modules) as well as non-credit-bearing courses (PG-only), taught by highly qualified and experienced language teachers, coming from a wide range of linguistic and cultural backgrounds with an expertise in the medical sector as well as in culture, politics, business, and international affairs.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 2\. Assessment

For most modules you are examined by in-course assessment and written examinations (there are exceptions, notably if the module is a Project). These assessments may be treated as formative assessment (intended to promote further improvement of student attainment - feedback is provided but a mark is not always given) or summative assessment (summarise student attainment at a particular time in their study - a mark is given and feedback provided).

There are a variety of ways you may be assessed, including:

* Written Examinations
* Multiple Choice Questions (MCQ's) Examinations
* Class Tests
* Lab Tests
* Coursework
* Projects

**A list of all Informatics assessment deadlines will be available here: Informatics Assessment Deadlines 2025-26.** ~~~~

Further information on the Informatics mitigating circumstances process can be found here: [How can I apply for mitigating circumstances?](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01744/en-us)
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 2\. Assessment

#### 2.1. Feedback on assessment

Feedback is at the core of learning and teaching at King's. In its simplest form, feedback is a conversation between student and teacher. It aims to be insightful, critical and enabling: feedback is an exercise in learning rather than a quantitative measure of how well you have done in your last piece of work. It is reasonable to expect that you will have received feedback before undertaking any major piece of assessed coursework during the year and all your formal feedback before the end-of-module examination.

The feedback you receive will come in many different forms, both formal and informal, including assessment grades, comments on work, conversations with tutors, notes to an entire class, and discussion with other students. Feedback is not confined to written work (such as exam papers or coursework essays), it is equally applicable to other assessed activities such as class practicals, presentations and performance. And when you are on a placement, in a laboratory, on a field trip, on a ward or studying abroad, you are likely to receive feedback in terms of your performance or acquisition of skills. Whatever form your feedback takes, it is a valuable tool in ensuring your you can improve upon your next relevant piece of work/activity. Those marking your work will be using a set of marking criteria - you should also use these to help guide your future assessments.

Feedback is a two-way process between yourself and your teachers. It is your responsibility to make sure you understand the feedback you receive and seek out a more detailed explanation if you need it. Feedback should be easy to understand and to act upon.

The full College policy on feedback can be found on the [Policy Hub](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/feedback-policy "Policy Hub").
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 2\. Assessment

#### 2.2. Expected timelines for receiving feedback

It is College policy that you can expect to receive feedback within 4 weeks of submitting the assessment, and you should be communicated if there is a delay to this timing. There are some exceptions to this rule, such as feedback on your dissertations is likely to take longer, and if feedback is due to be given to you over public holidays. Further information on the policy can be found on the [Policy Hub](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/feedback-policy).
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 2\. Assessment

#### 2.3. Use of Generative AI

The below sets out the Department's policy on student use of generative AI in teaching and assessments.

Students should make themselves aware of the general College guidance on the use of [generative AI](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/about/strategy/learning-and-teaching/ai-guidance/student-guidance) in combination with the department specific policy.

##### **5 Models**

The Department has 5 models and each assessment will be attributed to one of these. The model used may vary between and within modules. There is no 'one size fits all' approach and it is dependent on the type of assessment and the learning outcomes that need to be achieved by that assessment. Module Leaders will inform students of the model used for the assessment within their module.

##### **Model 0: Unauthorised use of Generative AI during Assessments**

Under this model, **no** use of Generative AI tools will be permitted. This is primarily for written assessments or lab quizzes where students aren't meant to have access to the internet, except for the purpose of downloading the quiz and uploading their solution.

**Model 1: Authorised use of Common Tools other than Generative AI**
Under this model, Generative AI tools cannot be used to correct grammar, spelling or translate one's own text to English. Only tools that identify and correct a text based on grammar rules are allowed. In contrast, tools that seek to change or embellish text based on its context or idea are **not** allowed (e.g., Generative AI tools deploying a Large Language Model (LLM)).

It remains the student's responsibility to ensure that the meaning hasn't changed.

To clarify, Generative AI or otherwise, may not be used to translate one's text from any language to English. This is because students are required to exhibit their independent ability to mount an argument on a given subject matter.

If the assessment is coding related, an IDE can be used to auto complete a function's signature, variable names and return variables. There is no need to reference the use of the IDE for that purpose. That said, under this model one cannot use tools that auto generate code (e.g., Co-pilot, Grammarly Business, Vertex AI, etc).

_'Do's' and 'Do Not's'_
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 2\. Assessment > 2.3. Use of Generative AI > **Model 0: Unauthorised use of Generative AI during Assessments**]

... the use of the IDE for that purpose. That said, under this model one cannot use tools that auto generate code (e.g., Co-pilot, Grammarly Business, Vertex AI, etc).

_'Do's' and 'Do Not's'_

* **Do not** translate your text to English. **Do** express yourself in English.
* **Do not** copy and paste text from any source. **Do** rewrite or paraphrase the main thrust of the argument in your own words.
* **Do not** quote Generative AI as a source. **Do** explicitly quote the text and cite the source.
* **Do not** use any Generative AI tools to embellish or improve your written text.

##### **Model 2: Authorised use of Generative AI tools to perform literature search
**

Under this model, Generative AI tools may be used to act as a search assistant, helping the author identify and gather relevant literature on a given topic. For example, one might use the following prompt "Latest research on DNA repair" and in response, receive a list of papers with a summary on each. Authors are still expected to have read, verified the authenticity, and discussed the content of any such reference they include, in much the same way as is currently done when using a 'regular' search engine. The author needs to be aware that Generative AI tools have been known to generate phantom papers. As in Model 1, this model requires the author to independently exhibit their understanding of the topic by critically appraising relevant papers, construct their own solutions and arguments or indeed generate the required artefact. As such, any verbatim quotes must appear in block quotation with speech marks. If the assessment was coding related, it is comparable to the use of refactoring and minor bug fixes offered in current IDEs. Under this model, an acknowledgement is required in the form of a brief statement identifying the extent to which the LLM tool was used along with the prompt.

Generative AI or other tools may not be used to translate one's text from any language to English or indeed correct, embellish or finesse one's own text.

**Model 3:** Authorised use of Generative AI tools to generate low-level output
title: Teaching & Assessment
heading_path: **Model 0: Unauthorised use of Generative AI during Assessments**

text:
[Context: 2\. Assessment > 2.3. Use of Generative AI > **Model 2: Authorised use of Generative AI tools to perform literature search]

... AI or other tools may not be used to translate one's text from any language to English or indeed correct, embellish or finesse one's own text.

**Model 3:** Authorised use of Generative AI tools to generate low-level output

Under this model, authors may feel an LLM is better equipped to generate a description or an explanation of a widely known topic or a concept. In such cases, authors are required to specify where such text/image was used, check the generated text's validity and accompany it with the relevant references after checking they are bonafide. To that extent, authors are required to have read all references they've used and if they include any text that was generated, it needs to include verbatim copying of existing work, and the author is required to acknowledge the source of the text and the source of the idea as well as include block quotes for verbatim copying. If the assessment is coding related, this will be the equivalent of using Copilot to author simple routines that are small in scale and carry out a specific functionality that is likely to have already been implemented by a library. In such cases, code must be accompanied by an acknowledgement in the form of a comment as well as a detailed explanation of what the code aims to achieve and its source (if available). Additionally, the author needs to be prepared for the option of being invited to a Viva where they will have to exhibit their full understanding of the generated output and how its behaviour might differ should a change be introduced.

##### **Model 4: Authorised use of Generative AI tools to generate new ideas
**
title: Teaching & Assessment
heading_path: **Model 2: Authorised use of Generative AI tools to perform literature search

text:
[Context: 2\. Assessment > 2.3. Use of Generative AI > **Model 4: Authorised use of Generative AI tools to generate new ideas]

... invited to a Viva where they will have to exhibit their full understanding of the generated output and how its behaviour might differ should a change be introduced.

##### **Model 4: Authorised use of Generative AI tools to generate new ideas
**

Under this model, authors may use Generative AI tools to elicit suggestion for a solution to a given problem or engage with the Generative tool to evaluate it as a tool for productivity. The problem or prompt is more than likely have been identified as part of the assessment set out by the Module Leader. In such a case, the author needs to acknowledge the idea as well as the fact that they've utilised a Generative AI tool. The purpose of the assessment under this model is to either further develop the generated output based on additional research, discussion, framing, etc, or produce a critique/feasibility study for it. If the assessment utilises a generative AI tool, equal access to all students must be ensured by advising student of the source they ought to use. If the assessment is coding related, it is the equivalent of auto-generating entire programs that are likely to span across many routines/sub-routines. In such cases, the student is required to ensure the generated output is free to use (note: sources such as: Copilot and Stable Diffusion are currently subject to piracy lawsuits) and fully acknowledge the source (if it is mentioned) as well as the purpose of the program in general and each routine. If there is doubt as to whether or not the code is free to use, it shouldn't be used. Additionally, the author needs to be prepared for the option of being invited to a Viva, where they will have to exhibit their understanding of the generated output and how its behaviour will change should a change be introduced.
