text:
### 2\. Assessment

#### 2.4. Undergraduate Reassessment

##### **Pass Mark**

The pass mark for Level 4, 5 and 6 modules is 40.

Undergraduate students in Year 3 of the BSc Computer Science programme or Year 4 of the MSci Computer Science programme can/will take Level 7 modules as part of their degree. The pass mark for Level 7 modules is 50.

If you don't meet the requirements to pass a module, you may be offered a resit or a retake at the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board.

Some modules have qualifying marks which means a minimum mark must be obtained in each piece of assessment to pass the module overall.

##### **Resit**

If you have failed a module (i.e., you have an overall module mark below the pass mark) because you haven't met the academic requirements, or you submitted late or didn't submit/attend an examination, you will normally be given a resit opportunity. Your module mark will then be capped at the pass mark.

The number of opportunities you will have to resit your failed module(s) is 1 resit attempt. Resit attempts are offered at the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board.

You will normally be advised on the format of the resit during the year. Normally, this will take the same format as the first attempt.

Formal examinations for the purpose of reassessment following the failure of examinations taken in either Assessment Period One (January) or Assessment Period Two (May), will be taken in Assessment Period Three (August).

Where you are required to resit modules in the next academic year, they can either be sat 'Out of College' or 'In College'. There may be specific requirements for some modules where reassessment can only be 'In College' for example, group and individual project modules.

'Out of College' resits means you will not be able to attend teaching. There is no fee if you take all your modules out of College, and you will not be eligible for funding by Student Finance England. Additionally, tier 4 visa students will not be sponsored for this period and will have to return home.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 2\. Assessment > 2.4. Undergraduate Reassessment > **Resit**]

... teaching. There is no fee if you take all your modules out of College, and you will not be eligible for funding by Student Finance England. Additionally, tier 4 visa students will not be sponsored for this period and will have to return home.

'In College' resits means you will be attending lectures, tutorials and you will be charged tuition fees on a pro rata basis, based on the number of credits taken (please visit: [Student Fees Hub](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/student-fees "https://www.kcl.ac.uk/student-fees")). Modules taken in-College will be attempted from 'scratch' - you cannot carry over any marks for assessments taken previously. Also, your attempt will stay the same. For example, if you are on your 2nd attempt, you will stay on a 2nd attempt even if you choose to resit in-College.

If you are intending to take your resits in-College, please contact the Programme team immediately via [informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk) so we can enrol you onto your modules and contact Credit Control to request an invoice to be raised.

##### **Retake**

There may be some instances if you have a failed a module that you will be required to take the whole module again. As per resits, your module mark will then be capped at the pass mark.

##### **Replacement**

If you have submitted mitigating circumstances and these have been accepted, your assessment will be deferred (normally until the next available opportunity). This means that the assessment will be classed as a first attempt and therefore your work will be marked as normal and will not be capped at the pass mark.

Further information on reassessment and can be found in the [Academic Regulations](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academicregulations/kings-academic-manual).

#### **_All undergraduate students_**

Reassessment of modules failed in the January and the May examination periods will take place during the [Assessment Period 3](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01393/en-us "Assessment Period 3") in August.

Reassessment of failed components of assessment will only be offered in cases where a student's overall mark for a module falls below the pass mark (40 for Levels 4-6). In this case, students are automatically entered for reassessment in August.

Once a module is reassessed, the overall mark for that module is capped at the pass mark. **Please note:** replacement first attempt assessments are not capped.

#### **_Undergraduate students commencing in/after September 2022_**

text:
### 2\. Assessment

#### 2.7. Submission guidelines

##### **How to upload and submit written work**

Details of how to submit work on KEATS can be found [here](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=2461953&chapterid=178354). To submit your work you may need to scan some handwritten work. Information on the recommended scanning app can be found [here.](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/pluginfile.php/4795851/mod_book/chapter/395561/Scanning%20notes%20to%20upload%20to%20online%20assessments.pdf)

It is important to be aware of deadlines for the submission of work for each of your modules. This information should be available on the module KEATS page, along with instructions for how to submit. This may differ for each module, or even for each assessment.

**You must submit your work by the stated deadline.**

It is important that you do not leave your submission to the last minute. If you are submitting online you are strongly advised to start your submission no later than one hour before the advertised submission deadline, so that you have plenty of time to ensure your submission is successful and correct, and in case you experience any difficulties in uploading the submission. The online tool (KEATS) automatically marks submissions as late by highlighting them in red to both you and staff.

Work recorded as being submitted ON the deadline will be marked as late e.g., if the deadline is 5.00 pm, the work must have completed its submission at 4.59.59 at the latest.

##### **Late Submission Policy**

All coursework must be submitted on time. If you submit coursework late and have not had an MCF approved for an extension, you will have an automatic penalty applied.

**For coursework where the submission deadline is 10 working days or more after the assessment was set:**

If you submit late, but within 24 hours of the stated deadline, the work will be marked, and 10 raw marks will be deducted. If this deduction brings your mark for the assessment below the usual pass mark (40 at undergraduate level, or 50 at postgraduate), your assessment mark will be capped at the pass mark. At the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board, and where your programme regulations permit it, you may be offered the opportunity to take the assessment again.

**For coursework where the submission deadline is 9 days or less after the assessment was set:**
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 2\. Assessment > 2.7. Submission guidelines > **Late Submission Policy**]

.... At the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board, and where your programme regulations permit it, you may be offered the opportunity to take the assessment again.

**For coursework where the submission deadline is 9 days or less after the assessment was set:**

If you submit late, but within 24 hours of the stated deadline, the work will be marked, but your grade will be capped at the usual pass mark (40 at undergraduate level, or 50 at postgraduate). At the discretion of the Assessment Sub-Board, and where your programme regulations permit it, you may be offered the opportunity to take the assessment again.

**All work submitted more than 24 hours late will receive a mark of zero.**

##### **Non-submission**

A non-submission of an assessment will result in a mark of **zero**.

If illness or circumstances beyond your control have prevented you from submitting, then it is advised that you review the [Mitigating Circumstances](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01744/en-us) procedure.

For full information about King's College London's Assessment Policies and Procedures regarding Mitigating Circumstances, can be found on [Student Services Online](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01744/en-us).

### 2\. Assessment

#### 2.8. Access to examination scripts

You can request copies of your exam scripts once provisional marks have been published by clicking [here](https://apps.powerapps.com/play/e/e3593fc1-b51e-ed0e-ac40-ded925ebff5b/a/5c83d5c4-6add-4c35-8059-b4c93deb8986?tenantId=8370cf14-16f3-4c16-b83c-724071654356&hint=dbc114ed-3834-4e54-b530-b1f95da53b1f&sourcetime=1748514704716). **Please note, the request form can only be used is a desktop/laptop browser, as it is currently not compatible with mobile phone.**

Please note that this request only applies to the following exam formats:

* Written exams
* Computer-based exams that had written/essay based questions only.

The following exam formats are **not** included in the exam script request service:

* Quizzes
* Class tests
* Group assessments and presentations
* Projects/dissertations/coursework
* Any practical components (e.g. lab work) of a module

Scripts will be emailed to you directly, usually within 14 days of your request being submitted.

You should be aware that it is College policy only to provide generic feedback, based on the cohort as a whole, for written examinations.

If you have any queries, please contact [nmes-examscripts@kcl.ac.uk.](mailto:nms-examscripts@kcl.ac.uk)

text:
### 2\. Assessment

#### 2.9. Appeals & Complaints

You have the right to appeal against decisions made by an Assessment Board on matters such as module results, final degree classifications and re-assessment decisions. Full details on how to make an academic appeal, along with the form and deadlines, can be found on the [Academic Appeals](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01218/en-us) page.

If you have a complaint, you should, in the first instance, attempt to resolve the issue informally and locally with the relevant person, at the earliest opportunity. If you're not sure who can resolve your issue, please speak to your Programme Officer ([informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk ](mailto:informatics-ug@kcl.ac.uk)for undergraduate and [informatics-pgt@kcl.ac.uk](mailto:informatics-pgt@kcl.ac.uk) for postgraduate).

If no satisfactory resolution can be reached, you may escalate the complaint. Full details on how to do this can be found on the [Student Complaints](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-01220/en-us) page.

#### 2.10. In-Person Exams

##### Before the Exam Day

Look on Student Services Online under the '[Assessments](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/category/?id=CAT-01058)' area to find various article about exams, how to get to the venues, what you can bring, what are the rules, etc.

Check your Personalised Timetable on Student Records and make a note of the following:

* Date & Time of your exam(s)
* Venue/Location/Room of your exam(s)
* Main venue is ExCeL London with some PAA students sitting computer-based exams at ExCeL as well
* Remaining PAA students will be sitting at the Waterloo Campus (usually in the Franklin-Wilkins building)
* The main exam venue is ExCeL London, Royal Victoria Dock, 1 Western Gateway, London, E16 1XL and is accessible from Custom House or Prince Regent DLR station
* Desk/Seat Number you will be required to sit in for each exam (will be different for each exam)
* Candidate Number for 2023/24 (will start with 'AE')

##### What to Bring to the Exam

* The stationery you need: pens, pencils, ruler, etc - the Exam Office will NOT supply these
* If allowed, a calculator which must be from the Casio Fx-83 and Fx-85 range. All other models will be removed from students desk - the Exam Office will NOT supply spare calculators
* KCL Student ID Card - if your ID card is lost please ensure you apply for a new one
* For computer-based exams, you may need your phone in case you need to reset your password
* Drinks - must be in a clear label-free bottle and non-fizzy. Alcoholic drinks are not permitted
* Note of candidate number and desk number - this should be put away prior to the start of the exam

##### On the Exam Day

**For Computer-based exams**

##### During the Exam

* Remain seated and in silence during the exam. If you need anything, raise your hand and an invigilator will come to you
* Standard time students cannot leave the Exam Hall in the first 75 minutes or the last 15 minutes of the exam, this includes toilet breaks
* PAA students are allowed to use the toilet at any time during the exam but cannot leave the venue until after the first 75 minutes
* If you need a toilet break - raise your hand to attract the attention of an invigilator. You must take your KCL ID card with you and register at the toilet desk
* If you wish to leave early - raise your hand to attract the attention of an invigilator. They will check your exam envelope is completed and allow you to leave

##### At the End of the Exam

* Listen carefully to the announcements made and stop writing when told to do so
* For paper-based exams, put your exam paper, answer books and any notes into the exam envelope and check you have completed the details on the front of the envelope
* Remain seated and in silence whilst everything is collected from your desk
* DO NOT use mobile phones or talk as you are still under exam conditions, even once your exam envelope has been collected
* You will be informed when you are able to leave, and you should do so quickly and quietly as other exams may be continuing
* We will ask you to leave in small groups to ensure disturbance to other students is kept to a minimum
* Please ensure you take all your belongings with you when you leave
* Remain silent until you are well away from the exam hall to ensure you DO NOT disturb others who are continuing their exam.

### 3\. Referencing

It is important to ensure you are familiar with the appropriate referencing system used within your department.

The Library provides comprehensive guidance on referencing through their [Getting Started with Referencing](https://libguides.kcl.ac.uk/reference) web page.

There is also an online course available through KEATS on [Understanding Academic Integrity](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/course/view.php?id=69502). All new students are encouraged to self-enrol and complete this course.

text:
### 4\. Marking

Your work is marked in accordance with the [College Marking Framework.](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/marking-college-framework) Assessment Sub-Boards select the most appropriate Marking Framework Model for each individual module.

All assignments are given a percentage mark. The pass mark for an undergraduate-level assignment in Years 1-3 is 40%. For Year 4 undergraduate (MSci) and postgraduate taught-level work the pass mark is 50%. Assignments will be assessed on the basis of understanding, depth of knowledge and structure (see marking criteria).

Some modules may also require a minimum level of attainment for a specific element of assessment (for example a coursework component) within your module. This is known as qualifying mark. Achieving this qualifying mark is a requirement for passing the module.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 4\. Marking

#### 4.1. Marking and moderation

All work that is submitted for summative assessment is marked by an internal examiner (with the majority of work then double-marked). To ensure marking is equal, consistent and fair the College operates a [moderation process](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/marking-college-framework).

The examiners and assessment boards in the Faculty of Natural & Mathematical Sciences employ a mark moderation process in order to ensure that students in the Faculty are fairly judged against the [College marking criteria](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/marking-college-framework).

It is a feature of numerate disciplines' exams (such as maths, physics and computer science) that the sum of the raw marks for correct results, reasoning, working etc. in an exam paper does not necessarily automatically correspond to the performance of a student when judged on the Faculty's and College's marking criteria. So although you might get a mark of 35 for an exam when all the marks are added up, the knowledge you demonstrated in your exam to get this mark might not correspond to the criteria set down for the 33-39 fail mark band. This is because exams in numerate disciplines are marked with much greater precision (with a detailed marking scheme and model answer) than in other subjects.

In order to make sure the final mark students receive always correctly reflects their level of knowledge and understanding, the Assessment Sub-Boards will define the raw marks that equate to the boundaries between different levels given in the Faculty marking criteria, including the minimum condonable mark, the minimum pass mark, the 3/2B boundary, the 2B/2A boundary, and the 2A/First boundary. This will be done by matching the corresponding levels in the marking criteria with the knowledge and understanding demonstrated in the exam papers. Using these defined points the examiners may then produce a translation when necessary which will map the full range of raw marks to the standard mark scheme given in the marking criteria and will give the final mark for an exam paper. This translation will be used to calculate your overall module result, will be reported on your student record and used for your final award calculation.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 4\. Marking > 4.1. Marking and moderation]

... raw marks to the standard mark scheme given in the marking criteria and will give the final mark for an exam paper. This translation will be used to calculate your overall module result, will be reported on your student record and used for your final award calculation.

The process of checking how the raw marks correspond to points in the marking criteria will be undertaken for every exam. It will be monitored by the Programme and Faculty Assessment Boards of examiners in line with standard assessment board procedures, and external examiners will ensure it is fair and in keeping with other universities' standards. The process is applied across a cohort to each module and takes place after marking once the total raw marks are available across a set of exam papers for a module, so it will not disadvantage or advantage individuals who have taken the same exam. Outside this process, as per the regulations, individual student's marks cannot be changed unless an error has been identified.

The process was introduced for undergraduate students starting from the 2012/13 academic year onwards because of changes to local regulations in the Faculty. Students who started in previous years have had other mechanisms in place to ensure they are fairly assessed.

The mark moderation process will ensure that each cohort of students are fairly and consistently examined across their programmes against the marking criteria, and in comparison to students across the College, and other universities

If you have any questions about this please contact your [Assessment Sub-Board Chair.](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=9124444&chapterid=921958)
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 4\. Marking

#### 4.2. External examiners

External examiners are senior academics or practitioners from other universities who help us to monitor the quality of the education you receive as students. In particular, external examiners scrutinise the assessment processes of each programme, helping us to ensure that you have been treated fairly, that academic standards have been upheld, and that the qualifications awarded are comparable with similar degrees at other UK universities.

Each external examiner submits an annual report with recommendations that your programme team are required to reflect on and address any issues raised in a formal response. The report and response are discussed with student representatives at the [Staff Student Liaison Committee (SSLC)](https://self-service.kcl.ac.uk/article/KA-02211/en-us), and are scrutinised by faculty and institution-level committees. You can access your external examiner report via the [External Examiners website](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/student/external-examiner-reports/index.aspx).

You are not allowed to make direct contact with external examiners, especially with regard to individual performance in assessments.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 4\. Marking

#### 4.3. Marking criteria

Marking criteria is used to judge how well you have completed the assessment. Your feedback should be referring you to the relevant marking criteria so you can see where improvements may be required.

The College has overarching [Marking Criteria](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/assets/policyzone/assessment/college-marking-framework.pdf):

* **Undergraduate Marking Criteria** \- pages 19-30.
* **Postgraduate Marking Criteria** \- pages 31-35.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 5\. Feedback

Feedback is at the core of learning and teaching at King's. In its simplest form feedback is a conversation between student and teacher. It aims to be insightful, critical and enabling: feedback is an exercise in learning rather than a quantitative measure of how well you have done in your last piece of work. It is reasonable to expect that you will have received feedback before undertaking any major piece of assessed coursework during the year and all your formal feedback before the end-of-module examination.

The feedback you receive will come in many different forms, both formal and informal, including assessment grades, comments on work, conversations with tutors, notes to an entire class, and discussion with other students. Although this guidance often uses the example of feedback on written work (such as exam papers or coursework essays), it is equally applicable to other assessed activities such as class practicals, presentations and performance. Some examples of feedback that occur across the College appear throughout this document, for instance when you are on a work placement, in a laboratory, or studying abroad.

Whatever form your feedback takes, it is a valuable tool in ensuring your next relevant piece of work/activity is better.

In a lecture another student may ask a question that you might not have asked yourself, and the teacher's response is feedback to you as well as to the person who asked the question.

Feedback is a two-way process between yourself and your teachers. It is your responsibility to make sure you understand the feedback you receive and seek out a more detailed explanation if you need it. Feedback should be easy to understand and to act upon.

The discussions and question and answer sessions that take place during a tutorial are useful forms of informal feedback.

##### Timeliness of feedback

It is College Policy to provide you with a deadline by which all feedback will be delivered; normally this will be no longer than four weeks from the submission deadline. To be effective, feedback must be delivered promptly, while you still have a clear recollection of the assignment just tackled, and so that it can feed forward into the next assignment. In all cases the timeframe in which you can expect to receive your feedback should be made clear so that you know when to expect it.

It is College Policy to provide students with generic feedback (based on the whole cohort's performance) on unseen written examinations.

##### Your role in the process
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 5\. Feedback > Your role in the process]

... you can expect to receive your feedback should be made clear so that you know when to expect it.

It is College Policy to provide students with generic feedback (based on the whole cohort's performance) on unseen written examinations.

##### Your role in the process

There is a core assumption that you will take responsibility for your own learning and this applies to feedback. However, learning works best as a two-way process and teachers are there to be of assistance. Asking for help may seem daunting in your first year at university but if you actively request information it is more likely that feedback in that form will enhance your learning.

Some forms of assessment (e.g. MSc dissertations, final-year projects, taped case studies, audio visual submissions, etc.) may require longer.

##### The role of self assessment

As you write your assignment consider the standards you are supposed to achieve and the academic conventions (for argument and citing evidence) to which you must adhere. Use this awareness to revise and improve your assignment before it is submitted. You should read and re-read drafts several times before you submit your assignment; this is good practice. In this sense the main feedback process taking place is with you.

Practice these skills for yourself by looking at previous assignments on topics similar to the ones that you are tackling. This helps to ensure that previous feedback is taken on board. A simple way to do this before you submit your assignment for marking is to review your own assignment in relation to the criteria that you have been given. Leave yourself time to revise your draft in the light of this self-assessment; don't hand it in without checking.

##### Self-supervision & self-assessment

The practice of 'self supervision' comes with experience and an awareness of what is degree-standard work and what is not. You can then bring this understanding to bear on the way that you write. In this sense the main feedback process is one of self reflection. The feedback from others acts as additional information which gradually increases the sophistication of your own self-supervision.

##### Asking for feedback

Teachers do not always provide feedback on those aspects of your assignment that you are most concerned about; do not be afraid to ask for specific feedback if this is the case. You should usually expect to receive feedback on your knowledge and understanding of the subject-matter at hand, and more specifically the strengths and weaknesses of your arguments, the factual accuracy of the material you presented, whether you have addressed the question/essay title sufficiently, and the appropriateness of the sources you selected to do the assignment.

Feedback may be a tutor helping you to recognise when you have (or have not) reviewed the literature thoroughly enough or have not drawn conclusions from the evidence that you have gathered carefully enough.

##### The role of assessment criteria
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 5\. Feedback > The role of assessment criteria]

... you selected to do the assignment.

Feedback may be a tutor helping you to recognise when you have (or have not) reviewed the literature thoroughly enough or have not drawn conclusions from the evidence that you have gathered carefully enough.

##### The role of assessment criteria

You should be able to understand why you received the grade you were given in relation to some kind of formal statement of expectations and standards. This enables you to know what you are aiming for and identify the areas that need improvement. If your programme does not provide a statement of criteria in the student handbook, then ask for it. Making best use of the formal feedback you receive will help you to meet the required standards.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 6\. Academic Misconduct

Plagiarism is a form of cheating and a serious academic offence. All allegations of plagiarism and cheating will be investigated and may result in action being taken under the College's [misconduct regulations](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academic-misconduct-policy).

It is important that, as a student, you understand that all types of cheating are unacceptable and will not assist your learning in the long term. You should also be aware that academic staff have considerable expertise in identifying plagiarism and have access to electronic detection services to assist them.

Note also that credit can only be given once for a particular piece of assessed work. Submitting the same piece of work (or a significant part thereof) twice for assessment will be regarded as cheating, even when the work was submitted as part of a different programme or at a different institution.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 6\. Academic Misconduct

#### 6.1. Plagiarism

Plagiarism is the most common form of academic misconduct, and may arise intentionally or otherwise e.g. through lack of understanding or poor scholarship. We define plagiarism as the taking of another person's thoughts, words, results, judgements, ideas, images etc and presenting them as your own.

Examples of plagiarism include but are not limited to:

* Copying: you should not copy someone else's work or thoughts and pass this off as your own, even if you have their permission. This includes using images and audio-visual presentations without acknowledgement;
* Incorrect referencing: you should not insert the writing or thoughts of others into your written work without the correct referencing;
* Copying and pasting: you may not copy text verbatim or closely paraphrase a sources text and pass this off as your own, without using quotation marks and citing the original source;
* Paraphrasing: you should avoid paraphrasing someone else's work e.g. by changing the words or the order of the words slightly, and should always acknowledge the source using the appropriate citation conventions.

##### College policy on Plagiarism

You are reminded that all work submitted as part of the requirements for any examination or assessment must be expressed in your own words and incorporates your own ideas and judgements. Plagiarism, that is the presentation of another person's thoughts or words as though they were your own, must be avoided, with particular care being taken in coursework, essays and reports written in your own time.

**Points to note:**
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 6\. Academic Misconduct > 6.1. Plagiarism > College policy on Plagiarism]

.... Plagiarism, that is the presentation of another person's thoughts or words as though they were your own, must be avoided, with particular care being taken in coursework, essays and reports written in your own time.

**Points to note:**

* Direct quotations from published or unpublished works of others (including lecture hand-outs) must always be clearly identified as such by being placed inside quotation marks and a full reference to their source must be provided in the proper form (your Personal Tutor or the lecturer can advise on this).
* A series of short quotations from several different sources, if not clearly identified as such, constitutes plagiarism just as much as does a single unacknowledged long quotation from a general source.
* Paraphrasing - expressing another person's ideas or judgements in other words - can be plagiarism if the origin of the text is not acknowledged or the work paraphrased is not included in the bibliography.
* It is possible for plagiarism to occur in examination scripts but particular care should be taken in coursework and essays and reports.
* Examples of other kinds of offence considered to be plagiarism are passing off work done for you by a native speaker of a foreign language as your own in assessed coursework, and repetition of your own work, if the fact that the work has been or is to be presented elsewhere (especially if it has already been presented for assessment) is not clearly stated.

Even in a minor or technical case, a note may be placed in your file for future reference. Further information is available online at the [Policy Hub](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academic-misconduct-policy).

Plagiarism is a serious examination offence. An allegation of plagiarism can result in action being taken under the [Regulations Governing Examination and Assessment Offences](https://www.kcl.ac.uk/policyhub/academic-misconduct-policy "Misconduct regulations"). A substantiated charge of plagiarism will result in a penalty being ordered ranging from a mark of zero for the assessed work to expulsion from the College, depending on the severity of the case.

You will also be required to sign and attach a statement to each piece of work submitted for assessment indicating that you have read and understood the College statement on plagiarism. Uploading work to KEATS (the College e-learning system) or submission by other electronic submission methods will also be taken as an acknowledgement that you have read and understood the College statement on plagiarism.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 6\. Academic Misconduct

#### 6.2. Other forms of Academic Misconduct
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
...### 6\. Academic Misconduct

#### 6.2. Other forms of Academic Misconduct

Self-plagiarism | Self-plagiarism is submitting material for academic credit which has been submitted, previously or simultaneously, for academic credit from us, or from another awarding body, or work produced by you for other purposes e.g.

published articles.

Previously submitted work may be included as long as permission to do so has been granted and where such work is properly referenced so that it is clear it has previously been submitted, or where resubmission of previously failed work has expressly been permitted.Examples of self-plagiarism include but are not limited to:Undergraduate or Postgraduate taught essays containing work undertaken at a previous institution, including A-Level work;MA, MSc or MPhil thesis containing work previously submitted in pursuit of the subject of the thesis (such as from an undergraduate or postgraduate taught research project);Undergraduate or Postgraduate work reproduced from work undertaken by you for other purposes e.g.

published articles, audit or other material in the public domain;Undergraduate or Postgraduate work previously submitted within another degree programme (where a protocol or an introduction is submitted for one degree and incorporated into a PhD final thesis).

Poor Academic Practice | Poor Academic Practice occurs where a student shows a lack of understanding of scholarly practice and appropriate academic representation.

Examples of poor academic practice are sources being cited incorrectly or inadequately or without quotation marks, even though the author is listed in the references.There are a number of different reasons why someone investigating a case of alleged academic misconduct might decide that the student's actions could be called Poor Academic Practice.These include:If it is your first offenceIf you are in your first yearIf the conduct relates to a fairly minor matter of referencingIf there is other compelling evidence that the conduct arose from a genuine lack of understanding of academic integrity expectationsPoor academic practice should only be found where the alleged academic misconduct is not extensive, blatant or does not result from an obvious lack of effort overall.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 6\. Academic Misconduct > 6.2. Other forms of Academic Misconduct]

... referencingIf there is other compelling evidence that the conduct arose from a genuine lack of understanding of academic integrity expectationsPoor academic practice should only be found where the alleged academic misconduct is not extensive, blatant or does not result from an obvious lack of effort overall.

Collusion | Collusion is when two or more students collaborate, without permission, to produce individual assessments that when compared significantly overlap in content, order, structure and/or format.Examples of collusion include but are not limited to:Unauthorised collaboration between you and other students to produce the same or substantially similar pieces of work which it is then claimed as your own;You submitting another student's work (in part or as a whole) as your own;Allowing another student to have sight of a piece of assessed course work before you have submitted that work yourself (where you may be submitting the same course work at different times in the year).

Cheating | Cheating is adopting working methods that are outside the spirit of the university regulations and involve acting in a dishonest way to gain an unfair advantage compared to other students.Examples of cheating include but are not limited to:Making up or falsifying data for an assignment such as a research project;Falsifying medical conditions or evidence to gain an advantage e.g.

deadline extension;Essay banks: where you can submit a summative assessment that has been written by a third party or obtained from a professional writing 'service';Taking unauthorised material into an examination;Not complying with the instructions on an examination paper;Not complying with the instructions of an invigilator;Copying someone else's work during an examination;Talking to other students whilst under examination conditions;Using unauthorised aids e.g.

calculator or a phone, during an examination when not expressly permitted.

Contract Cheating | Includes: purchasing or commissioning an assessment from a professional writing service or third party and presenting it as your own.

It also includes the use of a professional writing service or third party to edit an assessment or parts of it to cause changes to the structure or content.

The College only allows the use of proof-reading to check spelling and basic grammar.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
### 7\. Terminology

This page provides explanation of a variety of teaching terminology.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
...### 7\. Terminology

This page provides explanation of a variety of teaching terminology.

Modules | Modules are self-contained, credit-rated blocks of learning and teaching that make up a degree programme.

An Undergraduate (UG) student normally takes modules equivalent to 120 credits in each year of full-time study, while a Postgraduate Taught (PGT) student normally takes modules equivalent to 180 credits if on full-time study.

Most individual modules are worth either (UG) 15 or 20 credits (equating to 150 or 200 study hours, including lectures, seminars and self-directed learning).

Most modules consist of lectures supplemented by at least one of the other activities listed in this section.

Lectures | These are formal talks and can involve large student groups.

Some lectures include students from other departments and those of you on joint degrees/interdisciplinary degrees will be taking modules in other departments alongside your own and other students.

In most modules learning is sequential; you need to have met and understood past material in order to follow the current material.

You are therefore strongly advised to attend all classes.The College uses lecture capture as an aid to your studies.

The use of this digital recording enables you to have a study aid for your revision, review difficult parts of a lecture that you would like to return to, improve the quality of your notes and allow for more independent study.

You should be aware that while the lecture is being recorded any input you have into the discussion is therefore included in the recording.

Tutorials | Tutorials are provided for students, and may be group tutorials and/or individual tutorial.

These are less formal than lectures and seminars and enable you to raise your own questions about course material, as and when they arise from lectures or coursework.

Laboratories (Labs) | Many modules have practical sessions, held in Informatics labs.

These are for research and practical work such as coding.

You will be supervised during your time in the lab and should be aware of any health and safety rules whilst in the lab.

Projects | A project is a research assignment, given either individually or collaboratively with a group of students, which generally requires a large amount of effort and more independent work than that involved in a normal essay assignment.

You will be required to undertake your own fact-finding and analysis, either from library/internet research or from gathering data empirically.
title: Teaching & Assessment

text:
[Context: 7\. Terminology]

... students, which generally requires a large amount of effort and more independent work than that involved in a normal essay assignment.

You will be required to undertake your own fact-finding and analysis, either from library/internet research or from gathering data empirically.

The written report that comes from the project is normally in the form of a dissertation, which will contain sections on the project's inception, analysis, findings and conclusions.Although the majority of your project will be overseen by a supervisor, there may be some occasions when you need to attend a taught session on project work.

Placements | Some programmes offer students the opportunity to attend placements as part of the programme of study.

These are opportunities for students to take their learning into a workplace and complete a defined period of study working in an organisation, with an assessment, contributing to your overall credit value at the end of your studies.

Study Abroad | The College offers students the opportunity to study abroad and has a dedicated team to assist students with this overseas study.

A typical study abroad experience may include a semester or yearlong study at a partner university overseas, or co-curricular activity such as attending overseas summer school.

This opportunity gives you the option to gain an international, educational experience as part of your degree.

Coursework | Coursework is set for the purpose of your learning.

This coursework can encompass a wide range of activities including, practical, experimentation, research and writing e.g.

essays.

This coursework is used to broaden your knowledge, enhance your research skills, and demonstrate that you can discuss, reason, and construct practical outcomes from learned theoretical knowledge.

Sometimes this coursework can be set collaboratively as a group of students, but mainly it is set individually.

Workload | This relates to the volume of work you can expect to undertake during your programme of study.

This will include work such as attendance at lectures, seminars, tutorials etc, as well as your own self-directed learning and assessment to be completed (see assessment for further information).The general principle for credits is 1 credit = 10 notional hours of learning.

You can therefore expect for a 15 credit module to have 150 hours of learning, and for a 30 credit modules 300 hours of learning.

This learning will be made up of a combination of lecturers, seminars, practical's, tutorials and self-directed learning (see below) and taken over a number of weeks.

In the majority of modules self-directed learning is the main workload associated with the module.

Self-study | This is where you are responsible for your own study in each module, without getting assistance from a lecturer or tutor.

After a lecture, study your notes carefully.

Work through the details slowly and annotate your notes in a different colour to that used in taking them; this can help with revision.
title: Teaching & Assessment

It is important to keep on top of each module by reviewing the appropriate notes before the next class.

### 2\. UG Buddy Scheme

##### Department Buddy Scheme

The Department of Informatics runs a Buddy Scheme for Year 1 students, to be paired with Undergraduate students from Years 2, 3, and 4. The scheme offers the opportunity to meet peers who are further into their studies, and for mentors to provide a more informal form of support than you would receive from the department directly. If you are interested in participating, please fill out the sign up form as we aim to launch the scheme during welcome week.

Please note that this scheme is only available for Undergraduate students.

* Fill out the [signup form](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/questionnaire/view.php?id=8665203 "https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/questionnaire/view.php?id=8665203").
* Read the scheme's [advice](https://www.kclsu.org/change/wp/register-buddy/) for Year 2, 3 and 4 buddies.
* Note that students participating in the Buddy Scheme should behave with respect, courtesy and consideration to their buddies, in accordance with the [Expected Behaviour at King's](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/book/view.php?id=9124444&chapterid=931265).
title: Support & Wellbeing

text:
### 3\. Local safety procedures

##### Local safety procedures

You should be aware of basic safety procedures - it is your responsibility to take reasonable care for your own health and safety. You should:

* Comply with all safety rules and instructions relating to your own work; if they are unclear you should always ask for clarification.
* Report unsafe conditions or activities to your Personal Tutor, local campus safety officer or Campus Help Desk so that remedial action can be initiated.
* Make use of any safety measures or devices provided for the work, including personal protective clothing or equipment according to any instruction or training.
* Not intentionally or recklessly interfere with or misuse anything that is provided in the interests of health and safety.
* Co-operate with the officers and representatives of the College to ensure the implementation and maintenance of this policy and any local policy and arrangements for health and safety.

It is important that you make yourself aware of the guidelines for [laboratory work](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/lsm/students/pgt/handbook/laboratory-safety/Laboratory-Safety) and for [DSE](https://internal.kcl.ac.uk/about/ps/safety/general/office/dse.aspx) (Display Screen Equipment) use.

**Department of Informatics:**

- [Gemma McAlpine](mailto: gemma.mcalpine@kcl.ac.uk), _Informatics office Safety Officer_

### 2\. Student Staff Liaison Committee (SSLC)

Every department has a Staff-Student Liaison Committee or Student Forum that meets at least three times a year. The SSLC (or student forum) is an opportunity for discussion between staff and student academic representatives. It's a great chance to work closely with staff to improve the student's learning experience, and a big part of how together we can make education better at King's.

##### What is discussed?

Student representatives feedback comments, concerns and ideas put forward by the student body. These should be a mixture of things you are enjoying and you feel work well and anything you think we can improve upon.

Staff may also ask student reps to gather specific items of feedback from the student body.

Possible topics for discussion include:

* your experience of teaching - lectures, tutorials and labs
* your experience of receiving feedback on your work
* Departmental policies
* Departmental and College facilities - labs, the library, etc.
* Departmental and student society events
* the Personal Tutor system
* the Departmental Office - communication and organisation

The SSLC should not consider matters relating to named members of staff or students, nor is it the place for students to air their personal grievances.

##### What happens during the meeting?

The Senior Tutor, Head of Department or Programmes Officers may be able to offer advice or an immediate solution to a point raised. When more investigation is needed or other members of staff need to be consulted, the actions will be looked into after the meeting.

##### When will Staff Student Liaison Committee (SSLC) meetings take place?

Please note that the above dates may be subject to change.

##### Staff Student Liaison Committee (SSLC) representatives

There are three different Staff Student Liaison Committees in the Department of Informatics - Undergraduate, Postgraduate Taught and Postgraduate Research. Each SSLC is a forum for staff and students to get together, discuss what's happening across the Department then respond to and resolve any issues that have come up.

As a rep you can raise issues on behalf of your fellow students, and your department can consult with you and ask you for feedback on new proposals.

As an SSLC rep you will...

* Proactively gather feedback from your fellow students
* Attend your SSLC meetings
* Report back your fellow students on the discussions that take place at the meetings and of any actions taken by the Department.

As a student representative you should always be representing the majority views of your fellow students, not those personal to you.

Whilst it is important to critique your department and the wider Faculty and College and to let them know when something isn't working, it is important that you feedback the positive things too. ** _We need to know what you're enjoying about your programme so that we can continue to develop the things you feel are working_** _._

Here's what a former rep has to say...

_"Being an SSLC rep provides a very real and exciting opportunity to relay the student voice to the department. In an ever-changing discipline like computer science, the SSLC gives a platform to discuss feedback about the course as raised by students. The Department listens to all points raised and they go towards making a genuine and valuable difference._

_There is a range of benefits in joining the SSLC. For me, it was the opportunity to represent the student opinion and make a genuine contribution to the college. Being an active member also helps when it comes to CVs and interviews because of the attached responsibility. Additionally, the perk of a free lunch does sweeten the deal!"_

##### How do I become an SSLC rep?

At the beginning of the new academic year you can nominate yourself for a position as a student rep. A vote will then take place and the successful candidates will be chosen. If you are interested in becoming a rep, please nominate yourself using the process detailed in emails that will be sent to you and [here](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/page/view.php?id=8116478).

### 2\. Student Staff Liaison Committee (SSLC)

#### 2.1. Student Rep Nominations

##### _Nominate yourself to be a Student Representative_

**Nominations open:** 18 September - 6 October

**Nomination form:[NMES Informatics Department Student Representative Self-Nomination Form](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=FM9wg_MWFky4PHJAcWVDVigOy3Ct87tJuEs_p0OJFiBURU5OTk9OSTZWRVI2UjNGQzg4U0VNT1BaWC4u&route=shorturl)**

**KCLSU Training Week (Student Rep Fest):** 22 October

Find out more [here.](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/page/view.php?id=8116478)

Student representatives are the voice of their peers and one of the critical links between students and staff. At the start of the academic year 2025/26 will be emailed a self-nomination form which details all the vacant rep positions open to you in your department, it will also contain information on how to nominate yourself for these roles. Positions are open to all students, and for the first time you will be able to stand again even if you have been a rep before. Positions will be allocated on a first come first served basis so make sure you apply as soon as the email hits your King's Inbox!

There will be a maximum number of 2 students who can occupy each position, please note that priority will be given to students standing to be a rep for the first time. We will be sending you further information about student reps and the role they play in the NMES faculty over the coming weeks but if you would like to know more or ask anything specific at this stage, please visit the Student Rep page [here](https://keats.kcl.ac.uk/mod/page/view.php?id=8116512) and don't hesitate to email me - bel.mcintosh@kcl.ac.uk

###### Job description

Student representatives at King's are the voice of their peers, one of the critical links between students and staff. This is a fantastic opportunity to work closely with other reps, King's College London Student Union, department staff and the Student Experience Team to improve students' experience whilst studying at King's College London.
title: Student Voice

text:
[Context: 2\. Student Staff Liaison Committee (SSLC) > 2.1. Student Rep Nominations > _Nominate yourself to be a Student Representative_ > Job description]

..., one of the critical links between students and staff. This is a fantastic opportunity to work closely with other reps, King's College London Student Union, department staff and the Student Experience Team to improve students' experience whilst studying at King's College London.

As an NMES student rep you will have a strong interest in listening to your fellow students, canvassing for feedback on a wide range of subjects and speaking on behalf of your community. It is an exciting prospect to be able to help develop solutions by working in collaboration with others as part of a team.

###### Key responsibilities

- To attend committee meetings as arranged by your department

- Request, gather and collate feedback from your fellow students

- Engage in meaningful discussions with members of your student community

- Maintaining respectful communication when presenting feedback

- Report back to your cohort

- Play an active role in the calendar of events planned to support reps, including the student rep training sessions (RepFest 30th October - 1st November) and the regular check-ins and meet-ups that take place throughout the year

###### Qualifications and skills required

- No formal qualifications required

- Enthusiasm for improving education

- Interest in diversity, equity and inclusion

###### Experience and skills you will develop

Teamwork skills, including:

- Building relationships across your community/cohort and developing lines of communication that help the NMES faculty further connect with their students

Leadership skills, including:

\- A balanced approach when dealing with challenging and difficult situations

Time management skills, including:

\- Organisation skills that support you in requesting and collating feedback

Public speaking, networking and communication skills, including:

- A methodical approach to disseminating data to help shape the agenda of topics for discussion, and presenting at Staff Student Liaison Committees (SSLCs) several times a year

\- An insight into the workings of committees and how issues raised are addressed
title: Student Voice
